target.title: feat(googlechat): add sessionThread option to bind OpenClaw sessions to Google Chat threads
target.body: AI-assisted: yes (Claude Opus 4.7). ## Summary - Problem: In Google Chat, all messages in a space shared a single OpenClaw session. Starting a new thread to change topic still inherited memory from prior threads, and replies did not reliably land in the user's inbound thread. - Why it matters: Chat threads are the natural topic boundary in Google Chat. Leaking context across threads produces confusing replies ("why does the bot remember something from a different thread?"), forces multiple concurrent threads to queue sequentially on one session, and breaks the mental model users already have from Slack. Requested in #13373. - What changed: Added an opt-in `sessionThread` channel option. When `true`, the OpenClaw session peer id is derived from the inbound thread name, so each Google Chat thread is its own conversation. Replies are now threaded via `thread.name` + `messageReplyOption=REPLY_MESSAGE_FALLBACK_TO_NEW_THREAD`, so bot responses land in the user's thread. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): Default behavior is unchanged (`sessionThread: false` keeps space-scoped sessions). No changes to auth, allowlists, webhook routing, DM flow, reactions, or attachments. No slash commands / `/focus`-style bindings added (see "future work" below). ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [x] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes #13373 (per-thread session mode feature request) - Closes #41788 (Google Chat replies creating new threads instead of staying in the existing one) - Related #44347 — resolves the first of the two requests in that issue - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ### Note on naming vs the issue's proposal The issue proposes `threadSessionMode: "per-thread" | "per-space"`. This PR ships the same behavior under a boolean flag `sessionThread: boolean` (default `false` = per-space, `true` = per-thread). Rationale: - A boolean matches the two-state nature of the option and is simpler to document. - The name aligns with the existing session-scoping vocabulary in the codebase. - Happy to rename to `threadSessionMode` with a string enum if maintainers prefer the issue's exact wording, it's a straightforward rename. ## Root Cause (if applicable) - Root cause: The Google Chat channel used the space id as the session peer id and did not pass the inbound thread name back on outbound messages. That coupling meant (a) all threads in a space mapped to one session, and (b) replies could fall back to a new thread instead of the user's. - Missing detection / guardrail: No peer-id unit coverage for Google Chat; Slack already has `prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts` as prior art for this exact pattern, but nothing equivalent existed for Google Chat. - Contributing context (if known): Google Chat threads were less prominent historically in OpenClaw flows; the space-scoped default was acceptable until users started relying on threads to separate topics. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [ ] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: `extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.session-thread.test.ts` - Scenario the test should lock in: `resolveGoogleChatPeerId` returns the thread name when `sessionThread` is enabled and an inbound thread is present; falls back to `spaceId` otherwise; and produces distinct peer ids for distinct threads so sessions do not cross. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The peer id is the single point where "which session does this message belong to?" is decided for Google Chat. Locking its behavior at the helper boundary catches the regression without pulling in the full monitor stack. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): None for Google Chat. Slack's `prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts` covers the analogous primitive. ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - New opt-in config: `channels.googlechat.sessionThread` (default `false`). - `false` (default): space-scoped sessions, prior behavior unchanged. - `true`: one OpenClaw session per Google Chat thread; a new thread starts a fresh conversation. - Outbound messages now consistently target the inbound thread via `thread.name` + `messageReplyOption=REPLY_MESSAGE_FALLBACK_TO_NEW_THREAD`, so replies land in the user's thread more reliably. This applies regardless of `sessionThread`. ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text sessionThread: false (default, unchanged) space S, thread A -> session(peer=S) space S, thread B -> session(peer=S) # shared memory sessionThread: true space S, thread A -> session(peer=A) space S, thread B -> session(peer=B) # isolated memory space S, no thread -> session(peer=S) # falls back to space-scoped ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? No - Secrets/tokens handling changed? No - New/changed network calls? No (same Chat API endpoints; only request payload now sets `thread.name` and `messageReplyOption`) - Command/tool execution surface changed? No - Data access scope changed? No (in fact, `sessionThread: true` narrows memory scope per thread) ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS - Runtime/container: Node 22 / local gateway (`pnpm openclaw gateway restart`) - Model/provider: any (provider-agnostic) - Integration/channel (if any): Google Chat - Relevant config (redacted): ```jsonc { "channels": { "googlechat": { "enabled": true, "serviceAccountFile": "/path/to/service-account.json", "sessionThread": true } } } ``` ### Steps 1. In a Google Chat space, mention the bot in thread A and ask an identifying question (e.g. "remember the color red"). 2. Start a new thread B in the same space and ask "what color did I mention?". 3. Reply again inside thread A and ask the same question. ### Expected - `sessionThread: true`: in thread B, the bot has no memory of thread A. In thread A, the bot still remembers "red". Replies land in the thread the user messaged in. - `sessionThread: false` (default): behavior is unchanged from prior releases. ### Actual - Matches expected for both modes. ## Evidence - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after ``` pnpm test extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.session-thread.test.ts ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns spaceId when sessionThread is disabled ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns spaceId when sessionThread is undefined (default) ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns thread name when sessionThread is enabled and inbound has a thread ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId falls back to spaceId when sessionThread is enabled but inbound has no thread ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId produces distinct peer ids for distinct threads so sessions don't cross Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 5 passed (5) ``` - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - When disabled(previous behavior): - When enabled: - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) - Verified scenarios: - Manually verified in a real Google Chat space against a local gateway: - Bot replies land inside the user's inbound thread (both existing threads and thread-starter messages). - With `sessionThread: true`: new thread = fresh session; prior thread keeps its own context; no cross-thread memory leakage. - With `sessionThread: false`: unchanged, space-scoped memory. - Edge cases checked: - Inbound message with no thread (thread-starter): falls back to space-scoped session as designed. - Two concurrent threads in the same space maintain separate sessions. - What you did **not** verify: - Bot-initiated outbound into a space with no prior inbound thread context (no webhook-origin thread). This PR intentionally does not introduce a `threadKey` fallback for that case; the follow-up work below can add richer binding if demand emerges. - DM flow (no behavior change expected; DM scope is already per-channel-peer). ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? Yes - Config/env changes? Yes, additive only: new optional `channels.googlechat.sessionThread` boolean (default `false`). - Migration needed? No. Existing configs continue to behave as before. - If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A. To opt in, set `channels.googlechat.sessionThread: true`. ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: Users relying on cross-thread memory in a space would lose that if they set `sessionThread: true` without realizing the implication. - Mitigation: Default is `false`; docs call out the per-thread isolation explicitly; the name `sessionThread` signals the intent. - Risk: `resolveGoogleChatPeerId` is a new exported helper surface from the plugin's `monitor.ts`. - Mitigation: Kept it narrow (one pure function) and it is only consumed inside the extension itself; no public SDK subpath added. ## Future Work (non-blocking) This PR adds the minimal primitive, matching Slack's auto thread-scoped session behavior, just gated behind an opt-in flag for Google Chat. A follow-up could align with the Telegram/Discord `session.threadBindings` model (`/focus`, `/unfocus`, `/session idle`, `/session max-age`) on top of this primitive. That work requires registering slash commands in the Chat API app config and wiring `SLASH_COMMAND` events, which is out of scope here.

target.title: Add WhatsApp DM message-tool reply mode
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2-5 bullets:

- Problem: WhatsApp DMs used automatic final-reply delivery while groups used `message_tool_only`, so DMs could not reliably show live `message(action=send)` updates.
- Why it matters: Users who want WhatsApp DMs to behave like live group chats need an opt-in mode without risking silent turns when a model forgets the message tool.
- What changed: Added `channels.whatsapp.dmVisibleReplies: "automatic" | "message_tool"`, per-account overrides, DM routing into `message_tool_only`, prompt guidance, docs, config schema coverage, and a DM-only suppressed-final fallback.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): The default remains `automatic`, WhatsApp groups keep their existing behavior, and send-policy denials still block fallback delivery.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #N/A
- Related #N/A
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`.

- Root cause: WhatsApp direct chats and groups intentionally used different source reply delivery modes, and direct chats had no supported opt-in for group-like message-tool delivery.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Existing coverage did not assert a DM-specific message-tool-only mode with a same-chat final fallback.
- Contributing context (if known): A prototype that simply suppressed automatic direct replies could leave users with no visible answer when the model failed to call `message(action=send)`.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should catch this. Otherwise write `N/A`.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor/process-message.test.ts`, `extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor/inbound-dispatch.test.ts`, `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: WhatsApp DMs opt into `message_tool_only`, groups do not use the DM setting, automatic mode remains default, and suppressed final replies fallback only when enabled.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The behavior crosses config resolution, WhatsApp inbound dispatch, and shared source-reply suppression, so targeted unit/seam tests cover the actual contract boundaries without a live WhatsApp session.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

List user-visible changes (including defaults/config).  
If none, write `None`.

Adds an opt-in WhatsApp config key:

```json5
{
  channels: {
    whatsapp: {
      dmVisibleReplies: "message_tool",
    },
  },
}
```

`"automatic"` remains the default. In `"message_tool"` mode, WhatsApp DMs ask the agent to use `message(action=send)` for visible output and fall back to a normal final reply if no same-chat message-tool send happened.

## Diagram (if applicable)

For UI changes or non-trivial logic flows, include a small ASCII diagram reviewers can scan quickly. Otherwise write `N/A`.

```text
Before:
WhatsApp DM -> automatic final reply delivery

After:
WhatsApp DM + dmVisibleReplies="automatic" -> automatic final reply delivery
WhatsApp DM + dmVisibleReplies="message_tool" -> message(action=send) delivery -> fallback final reply only if no same-chat send happened
WhatsApp group -> existing message_tool_only behavior
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS local checkout
- Runtime/container: Node 22 / pnpm
- Model/provider: mocked test resolver
- Integration/channel (if any): WhatsApp
- Relevant config (redacted): `channels.whatsapp.dmVisibleReplies: "message_tool"`

### Steps

1. Configure WhatsApp DM visible replies with `dmVisibleReplies: "message_tool"`.
2. Process a direct WhatsApp inbound message.
3. Verify shared dispatch receives `sourceReplyDeliveryMode: "message_tool_only"`.
4. Verify a normal final reply is delivered only as the DM fallback when no same-chat message-tool send occurred.

### Expected

- DMs default to automatic final delivery.
- DMs opt into message-tool-only visible replies with a safety fallback.
- Groups remain unchanged.

### Actual

- Matches expected in targeted tests.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Targeted local checks passed:

```bash
pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 <touched-ts-files>
pnpm test extensions/whatsapp/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/accounts.test.ts src/config/zod-schema.providers-whatsapp.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor/inbound-dispatch.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor/process-message.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts src/agents/system-prompt.test.ts
pnpm config:schema:check
pnpm config:docs:check
git diff --check
```

`pnpm check:changed` was not run because the local Testbox CLI was unavailable during implementation.

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: Config schema accepts root/account `dmVisibleReplies`; account resolution inherits/overrides it; WhatsApp DM processing passes `message_tool_only`; fallback delivers suppressed final replies when enabled.
- Edge cases checked: Default direct chats remain automatic; groups do not inherit the DM-only setting; empty/silent/error suppressed finals are not fallback-eligible.
- What you did **not** verify: Live WhatsApp/Baileys end-to-end delivery; Testbox `pnpm check:changed`.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: Existing configs need no changes. To opt in, set `channels.whatsapp.dmVisibleReplies: "message_tool"` globally or under a WhatsApp account.

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`.

- Risk: Models may still return final assistant text instead of using `message(action=send)`.
  - Mitigation: WhatsApp DMs in this mode keep a same-chat final-reply fallback, and the system prompt now explicitly says visible output requires `message(action=send)`.

target.title: feat(anthropic): surface Claude subscription path in onboard wizard
target.body:
## Summary

- Renames the `anthropic-cli` auth choice to **"Claude subscription (no API key needed)"** and bumps `assistantPriority` to `-50` so Pro/Max users see the headless `claude -p` flow ahead of API-key paths in `openclaw onboard`.
- Adds `extensions/anthropic/cli-install.ts` so the wizard now (1) detects whether the `claude` binary is on PATH, (2) offers to run `npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code` after an explicit confirm prompt, and (3) launches `claude /login` interactively when the CLI is installed but signed out — instead of failing setup with a manual instruction.
- Web dashboard model picker and thinking-level selector keep working unchanged: `buildAnthropicCliMigrationResult` already seeds the canonical `anthropic/claude-*` allowlist and the existing `sessions.patch { thinkingLevel }` flow injects the directive into the system prompt sent to `claude -p`. Documented in `docs/providers/anthropic.md`.

Why this matters: today users with an existing Claude Pro/Max subscription either don't notice the `anthropic-cli` choice (its label said "Anthropic Claude CLI / Reuse a local Claude CLI login"), or they see it and bounce because Claude CLI isn't installed/signed in yet. After this PR the option reads as a first-class subscription path and the wizard handles install + login itself.

This is intentionally Claude-only as PR1 of a planned 3-PR series. PR2 will mirror the same flow for Gemini CLI; PR3 will surface a per-agent thinking-level default and a few quality-of-life tweaks in the dashboard.

## Test plan

- [ ] `pnpm install`
- [ ] `pnpm test:extension anthropic` — covers the new `cli-install.test.ts` (detect / install / login deps injected) and the updated `cli-migration.test.ts` (install declined / login OK / login refused / non-interactive error message).
- [ ] `pnpm build && pnpm check`
- [ ] Manual: run `openclaw onboard` on a fresh host without `claude` installed → expect "Claude subscription (no API key needed)" near the top of the Anthropic group, then the install confirm + `claude /login` prompts, then the model picker, then a working agent run.
- [ ] Manual: open the OpenClaw web dashboard, switch model and thinking level on a session backed by `claude-cli`, send a message, confirm the directive lands in the next `claude -p` turn.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: fix(agents): guard promptActiveSession against pre-aborted signal
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem**: When `runAbortController.signal` is already aborted before `promptActiveSession()` is called, JS evaluates `activeSession.prompt()` first (starting a new `Agent._runLoop` with a fresh `abortController`), then `abortable()` rejects immediately. The floating Promise creates a zombie loop that never terminates. Observed in production: 2617 LLM calls over 103 minutes from a single zombie run.
- **Why it matters**: Silent unbounded LLM cost; no user-visible symptom. Triggered deterministically by `queue.mode: interrupt` + rapid consecutive messages, and probabilistically by timeout-compaction retries.
- **What changed**: A pre-prompt guard inside `promptActiveSession` checks `runAbortController.signal.aborted` and throws an AbortError before `activeSession.prompt()` is ever called, preventing the floating Promise entirely.
- **What did NOT change**: No behavior change for normal (non-aborted) runs. No config added. Does not modify `abortable.ts` or any other shared utility. Complements #80239 (abort-settle cleanup ordering) by eliminating the root cause at the call site rather than mitigating the aftermath.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #74859
- Related #80239
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- **Root cause**: `abortable(trackPromptSettlePromise(activeSession.prompt(prompt, options)))` — JS evaluates `activeSession.prompt()` before `abortable()` runs its abort check. When the signal is pre-aborted, `prompt()` starts a new `_runLoop` with a fresh `abortController` that is never signaled.
- **Missing detection / guardrail**: No pre-call abort state check before `prompt()` inside `promptActiveSession`.
- **Contributing context**: #80239 added `trackPromptSettlePromise` and abort-settle cleanup but did not add a pre-prompt guard — the zombie loop could still be created, though cleanup now waits for it to settle (bounded 2s timeout).

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level: [x] Unit test
- Target file: `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.pre-abort-guard.test.ts`
- Scenarios locked in:
  1. Pre-aborted signal → `prompt()` is never called → zero LLM calls
  2. Normal (non-aborted) signal → `prompt()` executes normally, unaffected by the guard
  3. AbortError preserves the original reason as `cause`
  4. Bug demonstration: without the guard, `prompt()` IS called despite pre-aborted signal (proves the bug exists)
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: Tests the guard logic directly via a minimal composition of `promptActiveSession` without booting the full embedded runner. Deterministic — the signal is already aborted at call time.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

None. Aborted runs already returned immediately; this prevents hidden background activity that was invisible to users anyway.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before (even after #80239):
  abort arrives → abortRun() → runAbortController.signal.aborted = true
               → promptActiveSession called
               → activeSession.prompt() starts _runLoop (fresh abortController)
               → abortable() sees signal.aborted → rejects
               → floating Promise: _runLoop loops indefinitely
               → cleanup waits 2s (abort-settle timeout) then gives up

After:
  abort arrives → abortRun() → runAbortController.signal.aborted = true
               → promptActiveSession called
               → guard: signal.aborted → throw AbortError (prompt() never called)
               → no floating Promise created
               → attempt exits cleanly
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux (Ubuntu)
- Runtime: Node 24
- Model/provider: Any (bug is model-agnostic)
- Relevant config: `messages.queue.mode: interrupt`

### Steps

1. Configure `messages.queue.mode: interrupt`
2. Send a message; within < 1s send a second message
3. Observe the first run's attempt exits with `durationMs < 50ms`

### Expected

- No LLM calls after the attempt exits

### Actual (before fix)

- LLM calls continue for minutes/hours after the attempt exits; `embedded run done` never appears for the zombie

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: #74859 — zombie Agent `_runLoop` continues making unbounded LLM calls after attempt exits when `messages.queue.mode: "interrupt"` aborts a run before `promptActiveSession()` is called.
- Real environment tested: Linux (Ubuntu), PR branch `fix/zombie-agent-loop-abort-before-prompt`, commit `2b20b7e041`, Node `v24.13.0`, pnpm `10.x`, source checkout on latest `upstream/main`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.pre-abort-guard.test.ts` — 4/4 passed in 1.10s
  - Code path review: verified `promptActiveSession` on current `main` (post-#80239) calls `abortable(trackPromptSettlePromise(activeSession.prompt(...)))` without checking `signal.aborted` first — the guard is absent, confirming the bug surface exists.
- Evidence after fix: terminal output from PR head:
  ```
  ✓ skips prompt() when signal is already aborted — zero LLM calls (5ms)
  ✓ preserves abort reason as cause (1ms)
  ✓ calls prompt() normally when signal is not aborted (1ms)
  ✓ without guard: prompt() would be called even with pre-aborted signal (1ms)
  Test Files  1 passed (1)
       Tests  4 passed (4)
    Duration  1.10s
  ```
- Observed result after fix: pre-aborted signal path produces zero `promptFn` calls; AbortError is thrown with the original reason preserved as `cause`; normal (non-aborted) path calls `promptFn` as expected.
- What was not tested: live end-to-end with a real LLM provider and `interrupt` mode. The bug's deterministic trigger condition (pre-aborted `AbortSignal` at `promptActiveSession` call site) is fully exercised by the focused unit test.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test before + passing after: `attempt.pre-abort-guard.test.ts` — 4/4 green; includes a "bug demonstration" test proving that without the guard, `prompt()` is called despite pre-aborted signal.

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: unit tests pass (4/4 green); pre-aborted signal path skips `prompt()` confirmed by code review; guard throws AbortError with correct `cause` and `name`.
- Edge cases checked: `signal.reason` being `undefined` (no reason provided) — guard still throws AbortError with message "aborted". `signal.reason` being a non-Error value — wrapped with `cause`.
- What I did **not** verify: live end-to-end with a real LLM provider and `interrupt` mode (will follow up in the issue).

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: The guard could theoretically reject a prompt that would have succeeded if the signal was aborted after `prompt()` started but before it produced output.
  - Mitigation: If `signal.aborted` is `true` at guard time, `abortRun()` has already fired, meaning the run is being terminated. Calling `prompt()` in this state would create a zombie loop — the guard prevents a strictly worse outcome.

target.title: fix(gateway): tolerate Paperclip metadata via adapterMeta namespace + paperclip alias on AgentParamsSchema
target.body:
### Summary

Closes #74635, #62102. Refs #69139, #72565, #75901 (earlier vendor-named designs that were closed without merge).

Paperclip and other external agents inject a root-level metadata blob when invoking agents through the `openclaw_gateway` adapter, but `AgentParamsSchema` is declared with `additionalProperties: false`, so the gateway rejects the entire payload before dispatch:

```
invalid agent params: at root: unexpected property 'paperclip'
```

Three earlier PRs (#69139, #72565, #75901) tried to land this as a vendor-named root field and were closed without merge. This PR lands the same outcome behind a generic namespace plus a backward-compatibility alias instead, so future adapters do not have to grow the protocol schema each time.

### Schema change (`src/gateway/protocol/schema/agent.ts`)

`AgentParamsSchema` gains two optional fields, both treated as opaque by the gateway:

- `adapterMeta: Type.Optional(Type.Record(Type.String(), Type.Unknown()))` - generic landing pad for opaque per-adapter metadata. Each external agent or plugin adapter writes under its own key (`adapterMeta: { paperclip: {...}, greptile: {...} }`); the gateway accepts the bag as-is and never inspects it. New adapters land protocol-side without growing the schema.
- `paperclip: Type.Optional(Type.Unknown())` - backward-compatibility alias for adapters published before `adapterMeta` was introduced. The currently published Paperclip adapter sends its metadata at the root as `paperclip`, so heartbeats keep dispatching while it migrates to `adapterMeta.paperclip`. The comment explicitly marks this for removal once Paperclip cuts a release that nests under `adapterMeta`.

`additionalProperties: false` on `AgentParamsSchema` is intentionally untouched. Truly unknown root fields (typo'd `idempotnecyKey`, `somethingElse`, etc.) still fail validation before dispatch.

### Swift bindings (`apps/shared/OpenClawKit/Sources/OpenClawProtocol/GatewayModels.swift`)

Mirrors the two new fields on `AgentParams` in all four codegen positions (declaration, init signature, init body, `CodingKeys`), following the existing `inputprovenance` / `cleanupbundlemcponrunend` pattern:

- `public let adaptermeta: [String: AnyCodable]?`
- `public let paperclip: AnyCodable?`

The macOS-side mirror was deleted upstream so only the shared one needs to be regenerated.

### Tests (`src/gateway/protocol/agent-params-validator.test.ts`, new)

New unit-test file covering both the new fields and the unchanged guard:

- minimal canonical payload accepted, missing `message` / `idempotencyKey` rejected
- root-level `paperclip` alias accepts object, string, number, boolean, null
- `adapterMeta` with one or more adapter keys accepted
- empty `adapterMeta` and `adapterMeta` with primitive entry values accepted
- both surfaces side by side (`paperclip` + `adapterMeta.paperclip`) accepted
- `adapterMeta` with non-object value rejected (string, number, boolean, null, array)
- truly unknown root properties still rejected (`additionalProperties` guard intact)

### Changelog

One bullet under `## Unreleased` -> `### Changes` describing the namespace + alias and citing #74635 and #62102.

### Local verification

- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1` on the touched .ts files: clean.
- Standalone `tsx` reproducer importing the same TypeBox schema body and compiling it with the same Ajv config as `src/gateway/protocol/index.ts`: 19/19 cases pass (covering the 8 PR test scenarios plus their primitive variants); see Real behavior proof below.
- `pnpm test src/gateway/protocol/agent-params-validator.test.ts` is expected to pass on CI; local run was blocked by registry access while pnpm tried to install the github-pinned `@openclaw/fs-safe` workspace dep.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: The gateway rejected agent invocations with a root-level `paperclip` metadata blob (the shape Paperclip rev `3494e84` actually sends against OpenClaw `2026.4.26 be8c246`), failing with `invalid agent params: at root: unexpected property 'paperclip'` before dispatch. After this patch, the same payload is accepted as an opaque value via the backward-compatibility alias, and the new `adapterMeta` namespace accepts the same blob nested under `adapterMeta.paperclip` for the migration path. The `additionalProperties: false` guard on `AgentParamsSchema` is still in effect for unrelated typos.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout of `jeffrey701/fix/agent-params-accept-paperclip-metadata-74635` rebased onto `upstream/main` at `f9e8e83a16` (current `main` tip); Node.js `v22.22.0`; pnpm-resolved `tsx@4.x`; OpenClaw workspace `typebox` and `ajv` dependencies as pinned in `package.json`. The reproducer drives the actual Ajv-compiled schema body from `src/gateway/protocol/schema/agent.ts` against the same Ajv configuration `src/gateway/protocol/index.ts` uses to compile `validateAgentParams` (`new Ajv({ allErrors: true, removeAdditional: false })`), so the verdict matches what the gateway would produce in process.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: After applying the schema, Swift binding, test, and changelog edits and committing as `63770c9d4a`, from the repo root:

    $ /usr/bin/git diff --stat upstream/main..HEAD
    $ /usr/bin/git status -s
    $ pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/gateway/protocol/schema/agent.ts src/gateway/protocol/agent-params-validator.test.ts CHANGELOG.md
    $ ./node_modules/.bin/tsx .validator-repro-74974.mts

The `.validator-repro-74974.mts` script lives at the repo root so `tsx` resolves the OpenClaw workspace `typebox` and `ajv` packages; it copies the AgentParamsSchema body verbatim and walks 19 payloads (canonical baseline, root-level paperclip alias as object/string/number/boolean/null, adapterMeta with two adapter keys, empty adapterMeta, adapterMeta with primitive values, both surfaces side by side, adapterMeta as non-object string/number/boolean/null/array, and two truly-unknown root keys to confirm `additionalProperties: false` still fires).

Evidence after fix: Three terminal captures from the local OpenClaw checkout described above. The first capture is the diff scope against `upstream/main`; the second is the formatter run on the touched .ts files; the third is the standalone Ajv-compiled schema reproducer driving 19 payloads through `validateAgentParams`-shaped validation. All three are copied stdout as live console output from this checkout, not summaries.

Scenario A - terminal capture, diff scope vs upstream/main:

```
$ /usr/bin/git diff --stat upstream/main..HEAD
 CHANGELOG.md                                                |  1 +
 .../Sources/OpenClawProtocol/GatewayModels.swift            | 10 +++++++++-
 src/gateway/protocol/schema/agent.ts                        | 13 +++++++++++++
 3 files changed, 23 insertions(+), 1 deletion(-)
$ /usr/bin/git status -s
 M CHANGELOG.md
 M apps/shared/OpenClawKit/Sources/OpenClawProtocol/GatewayModels.swift
 M src/gateway/protocol/schema/agent.ts
?? src/gateway/protocol/agent-params-validator.test.ts
```

Scenario B - terminal capture, formatter check:

```
$ pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/gateway/protocol/schema/agent.ts src/gateway/protocol/agent-params-validator.test.ts CHANGELOG.md
Checking formatting...
All matched files use the correct format.
Finished in 4233ms on 3 files using 1 threads.
```

Scenario C - terminal capture, Ajv-compiled schema reproducer driving 19 payloads:

```
$ ./node_modules/.bin/tsx .validator-repro-74974.mts
Section 1 - canonical baseline
  PASS  minimal canonical payload accepted  -> accept
  PASS  missing idempotencyKey rejected  -> reject
  PASS  missing message rejected  -> reject

Section 2 - root-level paperclip alias
  PASS  paperclip object metadata accepted  -> accept
  PASS  paperclip string accepted  -> accept
  PASS  paperclip number accepted  -> accept
  PASS  paperclip boolean accepted  -> accept
  PASS  paperclip null accepted  -> accept

Section 3 - adapterMeta namespace
  PASS  adapterMeta with two adapter keys accepted  -> accept
  PASS  empty adapterMeta accepted  -> accept
  PASS  adapterMeta with primitive entry values accepted  -> accept
  PASS  both surfaces side by side (paperclip + adapterMeta.paperclip) accepted  -> accept

Section 4 - adapterMeta non-object rejected
  PASS  adapterMeta string rejected  -> reject
  PASS  adapterMeta number rejected  -> reject
  PASS  adapterMeta boolean rejected  -> reject
  PASS  adapterMeta null rejected  -> reject
  PASS  adapterMeta array rejected  -> reject

Section 5 - additionalProperties guard intact
  PASS  unknown root property somethingElse rejected  -> reject
  PASS  typo idempotnecyKey at root rejected  -> reject

19 passed, 0 failed
```

Observed result after fix: Live stdout from the three terminal captures above, copied from a real OpenClaw checkout running on Node.js v22.22.0. The diff is exactly 4 files (one new test, three edits, +23/-1) so there is no unrelated churn. The formatter reports the touched .ts files are correctly formatted. The 19-case Ajv reproducer lands every verdict on the expected outcome: the existing root-level `paperclip` blob (the shape #74635 reports Paperclip sending) is now accepted as an opaque alias for any JSON value, the new `adapterMeta` namespace accepts one or more adapter keys (Paperclip, Greptile, primitive values, empty object), both surfaces validate side by side without conflict, `adapterMeta` with a non-object value is rejected by the `Type.Record(Type.String(), Type.Unknown())` typing, and the `additionalProperties: false` guard still fails truly-unknown keys (`somethingElse`, the typo `idempotnecyKey`). End-to-end this means the failing trace from #74635 stops failing while no other root field becomes implicitly tolerated by the new opaque fields.

What was not tested: The full `pnpm test` suite was not exercised locally, because `pnpm install` cannot reach the github-pinned `@openclaw/fs-safe` workspace dependency from this network (`ETIMEDOUT`); CI on this PR will run the new `agent-params-validator.test.ts` file against the real `validateAgentParams` export from `src/gateway/protocol/index.ts`. Live Paperclip end-to-end dispatch (rev `3494e84` + OpenClaw `2026.4.26 be8c246`) was not re-run from this checkout, since #74635 already documents the failing trace and the same `paperclip: {...}` blob from that report is what the validator-test `Section 2 - paperclip object metadata accepted` exercises verbatim through the real Ajv-compiled schema.

target.title: fix(agents): prevent provider defaultModel from overriding agents.defaults.model (fixes #24170)
target.body:
When creating a new agent via 'openclaw agents add', the provider's defaultModel was incorrectly being set as the agent's model.primary, instead of inheriting from agents.defaults.model.

Changes:
- applyAuthChoiceLoadedPluginProvider: no longer returns agentModelOverride when setDefaultModel is false
- applyAuthChoicePluginProvider: same fix, removed misleading note
- applyDefaultModelChoice: same fix, removed agentModelOverride return and noteAgentModel call when setDefaultModel is false

This ensures new agents inherit the configured default model instead of silently using the provider's fallback model.

Fixes #24170

target.title: Fix legacy LLM timeout diagnostics
target.body:
## Summary
- Preserve the numeric `agents.defaults.llm.idleTimeoutSeconds` value in `doctor --fix` output instead of silently dropping it.
- Add a one-shot config-load warning when `agents.defaults.llm` is still present, pointing users to `models.providers.<id>.timeoutSeconds`.
- Cover doctor migration, partial-validation migration, and config-load diagnostics with focused tests.

Fixes #74910

## Real behavior proof
**Behavior or issue addressed:** Legacy `agents.defaults.llm.idleTimeoutSeconds` was removed by `doctor --fix` with only a generic message, so users lost the old timeout value and had no actionable provider-specific migration hint.

**Real environment tested:** Local macOS OpenClaw checkout at `82e9c03`, run with isolated temporary `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH` and `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`. The temp config contained `agents.defaults.llm.idleTimeoutSeconds: 180` and `models.providers.mlx`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
```sh
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=<tmp>/openclaw.json OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=<tmp>/state pnpm openclaw health
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=<tmp>/openclaw.json OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=<tmp>/state pnpm openclaw doctor --fix
```

**Evidence after fix:** Copied live terminal output from the real CLI run:
```text
$ OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=<tmp>/openclaw.json OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=<tmp>/state pnpm openclaw health
Config invalid
Problem:
  - agents.defaults: Unrecognized key: "llm"
Legacy config keys detected:
  - agents.defaults.llm: agents.defaults.llm is legacy; use models.providers.<id>.timeoutSeconds for slow model/provider timeouts. Run "openclaw doctor --fix".

Run: openclaw doctor --fix

$ OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=<tmp>/openclaw.json OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=<tmp>/state pnpm openclaw doctor --fix
Legacy config keys detected
  - agents.defaults.llm: agents.defaults.llm is legacy; use models.providers.<id>.timeoutSeconds for slow model/provider timeouts. Run "openclaw doctor --fix".

Doctor changes
  Removed agents.defaults.llm.idleTimeoutSeconds: 180; to preserve this behavior, set models.providers.<id>.timeoutSeconds: 180 for slow providers. Configured providers: mlx.
```

**Observed result after fix:** The real CLI now preserves the old timeout value in the doctor migration output and gives the user the exact provider-level replacement value to configure: `models.providers.<id>.timeoutSeconds: 180`. It also shows the configured provider hint: `Configured providers: mlx.`

**What was not tested:** No known gaps for this diagnostic-only change; no live LLM request was needed because the fix changes config migration and diagnostics only.

## Tests
- `pnpm test src/commands/doctor/shared/legacy-config-migrate.test.ts src/commands/doctor/shared/legacy-config-migrate.validation.test.ts src/config/io.compat.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/commands/doctor/shared/legacy-config-migrations.runtime.agents.ts src/commands/doctor/shared/legacy-config-migrate.test.ts src/commands/doctor/shared/legacy-config-migrate.validation.test.ts src/config/io.ts src/config/io.compat.test.ts`

target.title: Add per-session runtime envelopes
target.body:
Refs #74632

## Summary

- add an optional `envelope` object to `sessions.create` and `sessions.patch`
- persist the envelope on `SessionEntry`, with `sessions.patch` replacing it and `envelope: null` clearing it
- enforce the persisted envelope before tool execution through the existing before-tool-call path
- recheck the envelope after trusted policy, hook, and approval parameter rewrites so final executable params cannot bypass the persisted policy
- fail closed when a session envelope lookup errors
- document the protocol field and add create/patch plus envelope matcher tests

The enforcement is intentionally declarative and additive:

- `allowedTools` / `disallowedTools` are matched against normalized tool names
- `allowedPaths` / `deniedPaths` are applied to common path-like tool params
- `bashCommandAllowlist` applies to common command params

This PR intentionally does not expose `networkAccess` / host allowlists. Hook-level URL scanning is not a reliable network boundary for Bash, SSH, or query-style API tools, so network constraints should be added later through runtime sandbox/proxy enforcement.

## Tests

- `pnpm test src/agents/session-runtime-envelope.test.ts`
- `pnpm test src/agents/session-runtime-envelope.test.ts src/agents/pi-tools.before-tool-call.session-envelope.test.ts`
- `pnpm test src/gateway/server.sessions.create.test.ts src/gateway/server.sessions.store-rpc.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:test:src`
- `pnpm check:changed`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check docs/gateway/protocol.md src/agents/pi-tools.before-tool-call.ts src/agents/pi-tools.before-tool-call.session-envelope.test.ts src/agents/session-runtime-envelope.ts src/agents/session-runtime-envelope.test.ts src/config/sessions/types.ts src/gateway/protocol/index.ts src/gateway/protocol/schema/protocol-schemas.ts src/gateway/protocol/schema/sessions.ts src/gateway/protocol/schema/types.ts src/gateway/server-methods/sessions.ts src/gateway/server.sessions.create.test.ts src/gateway/server.sessions.store-rpc.test.ts src/gateway/sessions-patch.ts`

target.title: [codex] feat(mcp): stage binary tool content for relay
target.body:
## Summary

Part of #87023.

This adds a conservative relay foundation for MCP binary content:

- stage MCP `image`, `audio`, and blob-backed `resource` content into OpenClaw-owned outbound media attachments
- expose those staged files through `details.media.attachments` so the existing channel relay extractor can hand them off
- add a process-local host-owned MCP media registry so only files staged by OpenClaw can bypass the external-tool local-path filter

The model-facing transcript behavior stays compatible: valid MCP images still remain image blocks, while audio/resource blocks continue to degrade to text summaries for providers that do not accept those block types.

## Safety

- MCP-provenance arbitrary local paths remain blocked by the existing external-tool filter.
- MCP servers cannot bless their own local paths; only host-staged media paths are registered.
- Staging uses the existing outbound media store and media-core MIME/size caps, plus per-tool-result attachment-count and aggregate decoded-byte budgets with sequential staging.
- Staging failures degrade to the existing tool-result content instead of failing the MCP tool call.

## Validation

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-tools.materialize.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.tools.media.test.ts --reporter=verbose`
- `pnpm tsgo:core`
- `pnpm tsgo:test:src`
- `git diff --check`

`$autoreview` was requested by the contributor guide, but no autoreview tool is available in this Codex session.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: After the relay staging budget fix, MCP `ImageContent` and blob-backed binary `Content` from a real MCP stdio server are preserved as OpenClaw-owned outbound media attachments in MCP content order, then relayed through the existing tool-result media extractor/filter. The same run verifies that unstaged MCP local paths are still rejected.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout at commit `6898797` on macOS, Node `25.9.0`, isolated `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/var/folders/sh/mmzyqnnn76z5hv4ncznq6j140000gn/T/openclaw-real-mcp-relay.Ubj3gs/openclaw.json`. `pnpm openclaw --version` started the real CLI and reported `OpenClaw 2026.6.8 (6898797)`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Ran `pnpm openclaw --version`, then ran a live `node --import tsx` smoke that started a local stdio MCP server with `@modelcontextprotocol/sdk`, connected via OpenClaw `createSessionMcpRuntime`, materialized the MCP tool through `materializeBundleMcpToolsForRun`, executed it, and passed the result through `extractToolResultMediaArtifact` plus `filterToolResultMediaUrls`.
- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output from the live OpenClaw smoke at the latest pushed head:

```text
OPENCLAW_REAL_MCP_RELAY_PROOF_START
openclaw_config_path=/var/folders/sh/mmzyqnnn76z5hv4ncznq6j140000gn/T/openclaw-real-mcp-relay.Ubj3gs/openclaw.json
openclaw_state_dir=/var/folders/sh/mmzyqnnn76z5hv4ncznq6j140000gn/T/openclaw-real-mcp-relay.Ubj3gs
media_dir=/var/folders/sh/mmzyqnnn76z5hv4ncznq6j140000gn/T/openclaw-real-mcp-relay.Ubj3gs/media
catalog_servers=binaryProof
catalog_tools=binaryProof/relay_binary
materialized_tool=binaryProof__relay_binary
model_content_types=text,image,text
details_media_source=mcp
details_media_host_owned=true
attachment_count=2
attachment=image:image/png:binaryProof-relay_binary-1---21a9874d-ef38-4a58-8886-f1eb83d67919.png
attachment=resource:application/pdf:binaryProof-relay_binary-2---f63ee74a-a48b-4ba5-b466-4d1c52bc1cf8.pdf
extractor_media_count=2
relay_filtered_count=2
staged_file=binaryProof-relay_binary-1---21a9874d-ef38-4a58-8886-f1eb83d67919.png:exists=true:bytes=70
staged_file=binaryProof-relay_binary-2---f63ee74a-a48b-4ba5-b466-4d1c52bc1cf8.pdf:exists=true:bytes=133
spoofed_local_mcp_path_allowed=0
OPENCLAW_REAL_MCP_RELAY_PROOF_END
```

- **Observed result after fix**: OpenClaw connected to the real MCP stdio server, listed `binaryProof/relay_binary`, executed it, staged one `image/png` image and one `application/pdf` resource into the isolated OpenClaw media store, exposed both through `details.media` with `source=mcp` and `hostOwned=true`, preserved content order (`image` before `resource`), and the relay filter allowed exactly those two host-staged attachments while rejecting a spoofed MCP local path.
- **Budget regression proof**: Focused unit coverage now verifies that MCP media staging is capped at 8 attachments per tool result and that aggregate decoded bytes are capped before additional base64 blocks are decoded.
- **What was not tested**: Native Telegram/iMessage/Signal delivery was not exercised in this local proof; this PR intentionally stops at the shared OpenClaw MCP-to-tool-result media relay seam and leaves channel-specific UX/product behavior for follow-up review.

target.title: docs: Document oMLX (Apple Silicon MLX) as memorySearch embedding provider
target.body:
## Summary

Resolves the documentation gap identified in #74732. Adds focused recipes
for using oMLX (Apple Silicon MLX inference server) as a memorySearch
embedding provider via the existing OpenAI-compatible adapter.

## Changes

- `docs/concepts/memory-search.md`: New "Using oMLX" subsection under
  Quick start, plus an oMLX row in the Supported providers table.
- `docs/reference/memory-config.md`: New oMLX example under Remote
  endpoint config, with a warning against the common `provider: "ollama"`
  mistake.
- `docs/providers/openai.md`: New "Local OpenAI-compatible servers"
  subsection under Memory embeddings, surfacing oMLX as a notable
  Apple Silicon use case.

## What this addresses

Per the Codex review on #74732, this is the **narrow docs-only fix**:

> Add a focused docs recipe for oMLX that uses the existing OpenAI-compatible
> embedding path, warns users not to configure oMLX as Ollama, includes the
> local /v1 base URL and model example from the report, and tells users to
> reindex after changing embedding models or vector spaces.

All three points are covered. No core behavior change; no new provider
adapter introduced. The first-class `omlx` adapter discussion (Option B in
#74732) remains separate and is **not** part of this PR.

## Verification

The configuration shipped in this PR was verified end-to-end on:

| Component | Version |
|---|---|
| OpenClaw | 2026.4.26 |
| oMLX | v1.3.6 |
| Embedding model | jina-embeddings-v5-text-small-retrieval-mlx (1024 dims) |
| OS | macOS, Apple Silicon |

`openclaw memory status --deep` after migration:

- Provider: `openai (requested: openai)`
- Model: `jina-embeddings-v5-text-small-retrieval-mlx`
- Vector dims: `1024`
- Indexed: `150/150 files · 946 chunks`
- Batch failures: `0/2`

## Related

Closes #74732 (docs portion).

cc @jundot — oMLX maintainer, in case the oMLX side wants to cross-link.

target.title: feat(bootstrap): support session-scoped extra files
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `bootstrap-extra-files` could only apply one global extra file set, and explicitly configured extra files were still constrained by the default bootstrap basename list.
- Why it matters: operators with long-lived group/session workflows need deterministic session-specific context without renaming files to generic `BOOTSTRAP.md` or mixing unrelated domain context into shared bootstrap files.
- What changed: `bootstrap-extra-files` now supports exact `sessions[sessionKey]` path lists, combines them with global `paths`/`patterns`/`files`, deduplicates while preserving configured order, and loads explicitly configured workspace files with custom basenames through the existing workspace guards and budgets.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not add channel-wide selectors, session-type selectors, tiered bootstrap policy, symlink expansion beyond existing boundary checks, or a new global `agents.defaults` schema.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #39120
- Related #73917
- Related #50876
- Related #61001
- Related #22438
- Related #22439
- Related #54315
- Related #62110
- Related #73401
- Related #73988
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

N/A

- Root cause: N/A
- Missing detection / guardrail: N/A
- Contributing context (if known): Existing `bootstrap-extra-files` already resolved configured workspace paths, but the public contract only supported one global list. The basename allowlist also made explicit custom files such as `BOOTSTRAP-SUPPORT.md` unusable even when the path was intentionally configured and still passed workspace boundary checks.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

N/A

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `src/hooks/bundled/bootstrap-extra-files/handler.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/workspace.load-extra-bootstrap-files.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in:
  - exact `sessionKey` paths are loaded only for the matching session;
  - global paths and session paths combine in deterministic configured order;
  - duplicate global/session paths are injected once;
  - explicitly configured custom basenames load normally;
  - workspace boundary and bootstrap budget checks still apply.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the hook test covers config/session composition, while the workspace loader test covers file resolution, custom basename loading, ordering, and guard behavior without starting a gateway.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): existing hook tests covered global extra file injection and subagent filtering; this PR extends that coverage for session-scoped paths.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

`bootstrap-extra-files` accepts an optional `sessions` object mapping exact session keys to additional workspace-relative path lists:

```json
{
  "hooks": {
    "internal": {
      "entries": {
        "bootstrap-extra-files": {
          "enabled": true,
          "paths": ["shared/BOOTSTRAP.md"],
          "sessions": {
            "agent:main:whatsapp:group:123": [
              "groups/support/BOOTSTRAP-SUPPORT.md"
            ]
          }
        }
      }
    }
  }
}
```

Global `paths` / `patterns` / `files` remain backward compatible. Matching files are loaded even when their basename is not one of the default workspace bootstrap filenames, as long as they pass the existing workspace path and size guards.

If the same file is present in both the global list and the matching session list, it is injected once, preserving the first configured occurrence.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[agent:bootstrap] -> [global bootstrap-extra-files paths only] -> [same extras in every session]

After:
[agent:bootstrap]
  -> [global bootstrap-extra-files paths]
  -> [exact sessions[sessionKey] paths when configured]
  -> [dedupe + workspace guards + session bootstrap filter]
  -> [session-specific context]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

Configured extra files are still constrained to the workspace by the existing path, realpath, and file-size guards. This PR does not add external path access or expand tool execution.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22 / local pnpm workspace
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted):

```json
{
  "hooks": {
    "internal": {
      "entries": {
        "bootstrap-extra-files": {
          "enabled": true,
          "paths": ["shared/BOOTSTRAP.md"],
          "sessions": {
            "agent:main:whatsapp:group:123": [
              "groups/support/BOOTSTRAP-SUPPORT.md"
            ]
          }
        }
      }
    }
  }
}
```

### Steps

1. Configure `bootstrap-extra-files.paths` with a global extra file.
2. Configure `bootstrap-extra-files.sessions["agent:main:whatsapp:group:123"]` with a matching session-specific custom basename file.
3. Trigger the hook with the matching session key.
4. Trigger the hook with a different session key.
5. Try a session-specific path that resolves outside the workspace.

### Expected

- Matching session receives root bootstrap context, global extras, and session-specific extras in deterministic configured order.
- Duplicate global/session paths are injected once.
- Non-matching sessions do not receive the other session's extra files.
- Custom basenames such as `BOOTSTRAP-SUPPORT.md` load when explicitly configured.
- Outside-workspace paths are rejected.

### Actual

- Matches expected behavior in focused tests.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** exact-session `bootstrap-extra-files.sessions[sessionKey]` entries inject matching session extras while global extra-file paths keep the recognized bootstrap basename allowlist.
- **Real setup tested:** local OpenClaw pnpm workspace on macOS, running the actual bundled `bootstrap-extra-files` hook handler against a temporary workspace with real files on disk.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `node --import tsx -e '<runs the bundled bootstrap-extra-files hook against a temp workspace with matching and non-matching session keys>'`.
- **Evidence after fix:** terminal output from that command:

```text
workspace=/var/folders/3w/hh0mlt3n7p91vspj8jc_nfs00000gn/T/openclaw-real-bootstrap-dBsTbc
matching=["AGENTS.md","shared/BOOTSTRAP.md","sessions/support/BOOTSTRAP-SUPPORT.md"]
nonMatching=["AGENTS.md","shared/BOOTSTRAP.md"]
customBasenameScoped=true
globalRecognizedOnly=true
```

- **Observed result after fix:** the matching session received the custom-basename `sessions/support/BOOTSTRAP-SUPPORT.md` file, the non-matching session did not, and the same custom basename was not injected through the global `paths` list.
- **What was not tested:** live gateway/channel E2E; no symlink policy changes beyond the existing workspace guard behavior.

## Evidence

Local validation:

```bash
pnpm test src/hooks/bundled/bootstrap-extra-files/handler.test.ts src/agents/workspace.load-extra-bootstrap-files.test.ts
pnpm check:changed
git diff --check
```

All passed locally. `pnpm check:changed` selected a broad scope because the branch is currently behind `upstream/main`, but completed successfully.

Stable release reference checked before PR prep:

```text
latest stable: v2026.4.27
published: 2026-04-29T22:12:19Z
tag object: 89159965a186b8fa94475dc7b2d5e1703206f368
peeled commit: cbc2ba0931468259f26a7c547131a06e03ca6c6c
```

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - session-specific paths are applied only for exact `sessionKey`;
  - global and session-specific paths combine and dedupe;
  - literal path ordering follows configured order;
  - glob matches remain deterministic within each glob expansion;
  - custom basenames load when explicitly configured;
  - outside-workspace session paths remain excluded;
  - arbitrary extra files still pass through per-file and total bootstrap budgets.
- Edge cases checked:
  - duplicate path in global and session lists;
  - non-matching session key;
  - custom file basename;
  - path traversal outside workspace;
  - missing arbitrary file diagnostics;
  - oversized file diagnostics/budget behavior.
- What you did **not** verify:
  - no live gateway/channel E2E run;
  - no symlink policy changes beyond existing workspace guard coverage;
  - no broad performance benchmark, because this is a small config/file-resolution path change.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

No upstream review conversations exist for this PR yet.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`Yes`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

Existing `bootstrap-extra-files.paths`, `patterns`, and `files` configs continue to work. Operators can optionally add `sessions` entries keyed by exact session key. No migration is required.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Custom basenames could make it less obvious which files are injected.
  - Mitigation: files must be explicitly configured and still pass existing workspace boundary and size guards; docs call out the behavior.

- Risk: Session-specific config could be mistaken for channel/type matching.
  - Mitigation: the contract is exact `sessionKey` matching only; broader session-type policy remains separate and related to #61001.

- Risk: Large session-specific files could increase prompt size.
  - Mitigation: existing `bootstrapMaxChars` and `bootstrapTotalMaxChars` budgets still apply, with truncation diagnostics.

target.title: fix(config): migrate legacy ACP stream keys on load
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #35957 by porting the stale ACP stream legacy-config migration into the current modular runtime migration registry.

Migrated:
- `acp.stream.maxTurnChars` → `acp.stream.maxOutputChars`
- `acp.stream.maxToolSummaryChars` → `acp.stream.maxSessionUpdateChars`

Removed as legacy keys with no replacement:
- `acp.stream.maxStatusChars`
- `acp.stream.maxMetaEventsPerTurn`
- `acp.stream.metaMode`
- `acp.stream.showUsage`

Also preserves explicitly set replacement keys and removes invalid legacy renamed values instead of leaving validation errors.

Supersedes #60824, which targets the old monolithic runtime migration file that no longer exists on current `main`.

## Verification

- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/commands/doctor/shared/legacy-config-migrations.runtime.acp.ts src/commands/doctor/shared/legacy-config-migrations.runtime.ts src/commands/doctor/shared/legacy-config-migrate.test.ts src/config/io.compat.test.ts`

target.title: docs(gateway): add external auth-proxy recipe
target.body:
## Summary

Adds a recipe page documenting how to use existing OpenClaw primitives (custom `baseUrl`, eager SecretRef snapshot) to build an external auth-injection proxy that closes the agent-self-read gap acknowledged in #11829 and #10659.

The page does **not** propose any core change. It documents a pattern users can apply today.

## Why now

`@steipete`'s 2026-04-26 review on #10659 explicitly framed current SecretRef + redaction + plugin hooks as **partial** hardening, and noted that native masked-secret / credential-provider boundaries are unbuilt. PR #9271 attempted a full in-core proxy and was closed unmerged at 15.9K LOC. PRs #12839 and #22744 followed similar fates.

That leaves users hitting this issue with no documented workaround. This recipe gives them one — focused on the surface OpenClaw already exposes — without staking a position on what the eventual native solution should look like.

## Field validation

Implemented and running on a 21-tenant Badclaw deployment (Hetzner CPX41) for the Anthropic provider. End-to-end verification:

- Agent container has no `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` in env, in `/run/secrets/`, or anywhere on its filesystem.
- A live chat completion through the proxy returns 200 in 1326ms (Anthropic-side latency dominates; localhost-hop overhead measured at <1ms).
- Proxy access logs show source-IP-based tenant identification working as designed.

Reference implementation linked from the page: https://github.com/coletebou/Badland/tree/master/badclaw/services/auth-proxy (~150 LOC stdlib-only Node, MIT-licensed).

## What this PR doesn't do

- Doesn't add a built-in proxy. That's a much larger conversation (#9271 territory) and would need maintainer alignment on shape.
- Doesn't change SecretRef semantics, OAuth refresh, or any runtime behavior.
- Doesn't conflict with the active plugin-SDK work in #59165 / #72548 — those operate on a different bridge (credential resolution, not request rewriting).

## Test plan

- [x] Mintlify navigation entry added under "Authentication and secrets"
- [x] Verified `docs.json` parses (no orphan groups, page reachable from sidebar)
- [x] Internal links to `/gateway/secrets`, `/gateway/authentication` resolve
- [x] External links to issues/PRs (#11829, #10659, #9271) verified live

## Related

- #11829 — Security Roadmap: Protecting API Keys from Agent Access
- #10659 — Masked Secrets
- #9271 — Earlier full-proxy attempt (referenced in the page as a useful but more ambitious comparison)
- The comment I just left on #11829 with field experience: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/11829#issuecomment-4342619242

target.title: feat: fire session reset hooks for daily and idle resets
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Internal hooks and `before_reset` plugin hook only fire for manual `/new` and `/reset` commands, not for daily (4AM) or idle-timeout session resets. This means `session-memory` never saves on daily/idle resets.
- Why it matters: 4 community issues report lost session memory on daily/idle resets (#10142, #31266, #50891, #43524)
- What changed: Wire both hook types into the lazy staleness path in `initSessionState()`, update `session-memory` registration and filter
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No new hook event names, no changes to plugin SDK API, no extraction/refactor — only `export` added to existing functions

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Memory / storage

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #10142
- Closes #31266
- Closes #50891
- Closes #43524
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: `initSessionState()` fires `session_end`/`session_start` plugin hooks for lazy resets but never fires `triggerInternalHook` or `hookRunner.runBeforeReset`. These only fire via `emitResetCommandHooks()` in `commands-core.ts`, which is only called for manual `/new` and `/reset` commands.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No test asserted that internal hooks fire for lazy resets
- Contributing context (if known): The lazy evaluation design means sessions aren't proactively killed — staleness is checked on next message arrival, a code path separate from manual commands

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `src/auto-reply/reply/session.stale-hooks.test.ts` (new), `src/hooks/bundled/session-memory/handler.test.ts` (extended)
- Scenario the test should lock in: Internal hook fires with `action: "daily"` / `"idle"` when session is stale; `before_reset` plugin hook fires for lazy resets; no double-fire on manual reset
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: Mocks `triggerInternalHook` and `hookRunner` to assert dispatch without side-effects
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): `session-hooks-context.test.ts` covers `session_end`/`session_start` but not internal hooks or `before_reset`

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

`session-memory` now saves session summaries on daily (4AM) and idle-timeout resets, not just manual `/new` and `/reset`. Plugin `before_reset` hook now fires for lazy resets, giving plugins pre-archive transcript access.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[lazy reset] -> session_end + session_start hooks only

After:
[lazy reset] -> internal hook (command:daily/idle) + before_reset + session_end + session_start
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Any
- Runtime/container: Node.js 24
- Integration/channel (if any): Any channel with daily or idle reset configured

### Steps

1. Configure a session with daily reset (default 4AM)
2. Have an active session with messages before 4AM
3. Send a message after 4AM (triggering lazy staleness detection)

### Expected

- `session-memory` saves a summary of the previous session
- `before_reset` plugin hook fires with `reason: "daily"` and transcript messages

### Actual (before this PR)

- `session-memory` does not fire — no summary saved
- `before_reset` plugin hook does not fire

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: Daily reset with stale session triggers session-memory save; idle reset triggers save; manual /new does not double-fire
- Edge cases checked: First-ever session (no previous entry), system events (heartbeat), fresh session
- What you did **not** verify: End-to-end with real LLM slug generation (mocked in tests)

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: `before_reset` receives the post-archive `sessionFile` path (after `renameSync`), not the original path. `loadBeforeResetTranscript` handles ENOENT by scanning for the latest `.reset.*` archived sibling.
  - Mitigation: This matches the manual reset path behavior. The stable post-archive path is intentional — it avoids racing transcript reads against the archive rename.

## AI Disclosure

- [x] Mark as AI-assisted in the PR title or description
- [x] Note the degree of testing: fully tested — 10 unit tests (7 new + 3 extended) + manual daily/idle reset verification
- [x] Confirm: I understand what this code does and have verified the hook dispatch logic, guard conditions, and fire-and-forget patterns

🤖 AI-assisted with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: fix(whatsapp): support edit and unsend actions
target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?

- WhatsApp message actions could not edit or revoke bot-owned messages.
- The old PR also routed edit/unsend through PN-only JID conversion, which could misroute LID-addressed chats.

Why does this matter now?

- WhatsApp outbound sends already use LID-aware routing. Message mutations need the same routing contract or they can target the wrong chat.

What is the intended outcome?

- Add WhatsApp `edit`, `delete`, and `unsend` actions for bot-owned messages.
- Keep `upload-file` support from current `main` while adding the new gateway actions.
- Use `resolveOutboundJid` for edit/unsend so message keys use the resolved LID JID when available.
- Validate edit text before forwarding the gateway action.

What is intentionally out of scope?

- Editing or deleting other users' WhatsApp messages.
- Live WhatsApp server compatibility guarantees beyond Baileys payload construction.

What does success look like?

- `edit` sends a Baileys `{ edit }` payload with `fromMe: true`.
- `delete` and `unsend` send a Baileys `{ delete }` payload with `fromMe: true`.
- PN-to-LID mappings are honored for normal sends, reactions, edit, unsend, and typing presence.

What should reviewers focus on?

- LID-aware routing in `extensions/whatsapp/src/inbound/send-api.ts`.
- Gateway action dispatch in `extensions/whatsapp/src/channel-react-action.ts`.
- `actions.sendMessage` gating and target authorization in `extensions/whatsapp/src/action-runtime.ts`.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Closes #14344

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related #67378

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

- This refresh addresses the existing PR review blocker about reusing LID-aware routing for edit and unsend.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: WhatsApp edit/unsend now use the same LID-aware outbound JID resolution as normal sends and reactions.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on Linux with Node/pnpm and the real WhatsApp send API seam; temp Baileys auth dir containing `lid-mapping-15555550000.json`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```text
OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 pnpm test extensions/whatsapp/src/action-runtime.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/channel-actions.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/channel-react-action.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/inbound/send-api.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/send.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/active-listener.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/connection-controller.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose
```

- Evidence after fix:

```text
✓ createWebSendApi LID resolution (issue #67378) > resolves PN to LID for editMessage
✓ createWebSendApi LID resolution (issue #67378) > resolves PN to LID for unsendMessage
Test Files  7 passed (7)
Tests  144 passed (144)
```

- Observed result after fix: edit and unsend calls send to `987654@lid`; their message keys use `remoteJid: "987654@lid"`, `id: "msg-2"`, and `fromMe: true`.
- What was not tested: live WhatsApp account behavior against WhatsApp servers.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: no linked live WhatsApp account was available in this local repair environment, so the proof covers OpenClaw routing, dispatch, gating, payload construction, log sanitization, and listener contracts, not server acceptance.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): the old edit/unsend implementation called `toWhatsappJid(to)`, so a PN target with a LID mapping stayed on `15555550000@s.whatsapp.net` instead of `987654@lid`.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

```text
pnpm docs:list
OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 pnpm test extensions/whatsapp/src/action-runtime.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/channel-actions.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/channel-react-action.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/inbound/send-api.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/send.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/active-listener.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/connection-controller.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose
pnpm format:check docs/channels/whatsapp.md docs/cli/message.md extensions/whatsapp/src/action-runtime.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/action-runtime.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/active-listener.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply.test-harness.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/channel-actions.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/channel-actions.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/channel-react-action.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/channel-react-action.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/channel.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/connection-controller.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/inbound/send-api.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/inbound/send-api.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/inbound/types.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/send.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/send.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/shared.ts
git diff --check
pnpm changed:lanes --json --base upstream/main
```

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- `extensions/whatsapp/src/inbound/send-api.test.ts`: edit/unsend payloads and LID mapping regression coverage.
- `extensions/whatsapp/src/action-runtime.test.ts`: edit/delete/unsend gating, authorization path, and edit input validation.
- `extensions/whatsapp/src/channel-react-action.test.ts`: gateway dispatch, current WhatsApp context fallback, no participant forwarding, and empty edit text rejection before forwarding.
- `extensions/whatsapp/src/send.test.ts`: listener wrappers and log sanitization for message ids and reaction emojis.
- `extensions/whatsapp/src/channel-actions.test.ts`: action discovery with `actions.sendMessage` disabled.

What failed before this fix, if known?

- The LID regression added here would fail before the refresh because edit/unsend used `toWhatsappJid(to)` instead of `resolveOutboundJid(to)`.

If no test was added, why not?

- N/A.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes.

What is the highest-risk area?

- WhatsApp message mutation over an existing Baileys session.

How is that risk mitigated?

- Actions reuse existing outbound target authorization and `actions.sendMessage` gating.
- Message keys are constructed with `fromMe: true`.
- Caller-provided `participant` is not forwarded for edit/delete/unsend.
- Message IDs and reaction emojis are sanitized before log emission.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

- Ready for maintainer review after CI completes.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

- CI is pending on the refreshed head.
- Live WhatsApp server proof is still not provided; maintainer or external validation is needed if live behavior proof remains required.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

- Rebased onto current `main` and resolved conflicts with current `upload-file` support.
- Addressed the LID-aware routing blocker for edit and unsend.
- Kept the deprecated `handleWhatsAppReactAction` wrapper with a deprecation comment.
- Validated edit text before forwarding gateway edit actions.
- Preserved/verified log sanitization for message ids and reaction emojis.
