target.title: feat(discord): add canvas-first Discord Activities support
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

If this PR fixes a plugin beta-release blocker, title it `fix(<plugin-id>): beta blocker - <summary>` and link the matching `Beta blocker: <plugin-name> - <summary>` issue labeled `beta-blocker`. Contributors cannot label PRs, so the title is the PR-side signal for maintainers and automation.

- Problem: Discord Activity launch/setup in OpenClaw required brittle manual component payloads, had weak agent guidance, and failed command deploys when Discord required retaining the Entry Point command.
- Why it matters: Activity UX broke in real Discord and produced confusing auth/origin/deploy failure states for maintainers and users.
- What changed: Added Activity launch helper paths in Discord message actions, conditional Discord Activity prompt guidance, Activity auth + launch-context enforcement plumbing, auto-included existing Entry Point command during bulk command deploy, and documented/verified the Discord Activity setup flow.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No new standalone backend OAuth exchange endpoint was added; OAuth exchange remains app/backend-owned.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #N/A
- Related #N/A
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`.

- Root cause: Discord Activity launch assumptions diverged from Discord runtime behavior (root launch path + Entry Point command requirements), and OpenClaw lacked resilient deploy/request handling around those constraints.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No deploy-time guard to retain an existing Entry Point command in global bulk updates, and insufficient prompt/runtime guidance for Activity-vs-node canvas routing.
- Contributing context (if known): Discord iframe/origin constraints and launch metadata requirements were not first-class in the earlier flow.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should catch this. Otherwise write `N/A`.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `extensions/discord/src/monitor/provider.test.ts`
  - `src/gateway/server.canvas-auth.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/system-prompt.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/system-prompt-params.test.ts`
  - `src/canvas-host/server.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: Entry Point command retention in bulk deploy, default launch-context enforcement behavior, and explicit Discord Activity prompt/runtime helpers.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: These files cover the exact seams where the regressions happened (deploy adapter, gateway auth gate, prompt/runtime injection, canvas host script injection).
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Expanded/updated tests above include new and adapted assertions.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

List user-visible changes (including defaults/config).  
If none, write `None`.

- Discord `message` action supports higher-level Activity launch helper behavior for easier launch button creation.
- Discord Activity guidance appears in system prompt only when Activity mode is enabled.
- `canvasHost.activity.requireLaunchContext` is now treated as default-on (`true` unless explicitly `false`).
- Canvas-hosted pages expose `window.OpenClaw.discord` / `window.openclawDiscord` helper APIs (lazy SDK load).
- Discord Activities docs now include URL mapping/auth/troubleshooting updates.

## Diagram (if applicable)

For UI changes or non-trivial logic flows, include a small ASCII diagram reviewers can scan quickly. Otherwise write `N/A`.

```text
Before:
[launch-activity button] -> [Discord opens /] -> [gateway root/control-ui or auth mismatch] -> [failure]

After:
[launch-activity button] -> [Discord opens mapped /__openclaw__/canvas] -> [activity auth + launch-context checks] -> [canvas loads]
                         \-> [bulk command deploy] -> [auto-include existing Entry Point command] -> [deploy succeeds]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
  - Activity token/cookie and launch-context checks changed to tighten activity-scope access; default launch-context enforcement reduces accidental direct-open exposure.
  - Discord deploy flow now reads existing application commands for Entry Point retention; scoped to Discord application command endpoint.
  - Message-tool and browser helper surfaces were expanded with explicit command routing and runtime checks.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local OpenClaw runtime (Node 22+)
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Discord
- Relevant config (redacted): `canvasHost.activity.enabled=true`, `canvasHost.activity.requireLaunchContext=true`, Discord account configured

### Steps

1. Configure Discord Activity URL mapping and OpenClaw activity settings.
2. Launch via Discord `launch-activity` button and observe canvas/auth behavior.
3. Start Discord provider and verify native command deploy behavior when Entry Point command exists.

### Expected

- Activity launches to canvas route and passes intended auth gates.
- Native command deploy retains Entry Point command and does not fail with 50240.

### Actual

- Verified in updated tests and runtime flow for this branch.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - Ran targeted tests covering deploy flow, gateway auth flow, prompt/runtime guidance, and canvas helper injection.
- Edge cases checked:
  - Default-on launch-context enforcement and explicit opt-out behavior.
  - Entry Point command retention path and 50240 handling path.
- What you did **not** verify:
  - Full live Discord OAuth backend token-exchange endpoint flow (out of scope for this PR).

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:
  - N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`.

- Risk: Discord deploy behavior differs across app setups with unusual Entry Point metadata.
  - Mitigation: Entry Point payload is sourced from Discord's existing command record and included only when missing from outgoing bulk payload.
- Risk: Launch-context default-on may block intentionally direct/manual canvas opens for activity mode.
  - Mitigation: Explicit opt-out via `canvasHost.activity.requireLaunchContext=false`.

target.title: perf(gateway): cache session, node, and cron list lookups
target.body:
## Summary
- add a short-lived cache for `sessions.list` results and manifest normalization lookups
- coalesce session-list cache fills and invalidate on session mutations/events
- add short-lived cache/coalescing for `node.list` / `node.describe` pairing-list reads, invalidated on node-pairing mutations
- add short-lived cache/coalescing for cron delivery preview resolution used by `cron.list`, invalidated on cron add/update/remove

## Evidence
- production profiling continued after the session/node-list fixes and still observed hot-path list calls while the gateway process was under load
- 2026-05-05 profiler sample saw `node.list` spikes up to ~8.1s and `cron.list` / `node.list` together around 1.3-1.4s during liveness-warning windows
- this PR keeps the mitigation scoped to short TTL caches plus mutation invalidation; it does not change production gateway deployment

## Local validation
- `pnpm format src/cron/delivery-preview.ts src/cron/delivery-preview.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/cron.ts`
- `pnpm test src/cron/delivery-preview.test.ts`

- `pnpm check:changed`

## Safety
- no production restart or gateway switch performed
- no private deployment identifiers included

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Repeated Gateway `sessions.list`, node pairing list, and cron list/preview requests now reuse short-lived cached results with explicit invalidation after session, node, or cron lifecycle changes. This reduces repeated Gateway hot-path scans while preserving fresh rows after writes.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on the repaired PR branch after rebasing onto current `main`; Node 22 and pnpm 10. Secrets, account names, machine paths, and deployment identifiers omitted.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  1. Rebased/cherry-picked the PR payload onto current `main` and resolved only the changelog conflict.
  2. Ran `pnpm docs:list` before validation.
  3. Ran focused Gateway/cron tests covering `sessions.list`, session row cache invalidation, manifest/model row enrichment, and cron delivery preview behavior.
  4. Ran `pnpm check:changed` and `pnpm build`.
  5. Started the built Gateway on an isolated local port with `--allow-unconfigured` and checked `/health`.
- Evidence after fix:

target.title: fix(gateway): bound connect frame auth.* fields at the protocol schema
target.body:
Closes #77977

## Summary

The connect-frame schema at `src/gateway/protocol/schema/frames.ts` accepted `auth.token`, `auth.bootstrapToken`, `auth.deviceToken`, and `auth.password` as unbounded `Type.String()`. The only effective cap was the 64KB pre-auth frame limit (`MAX_PREAUTH_PAYLOAD_BYTES`).

`safeEqualSecret` (`src/security/secret-equal.ts`) pads both operands to `Math.max(provided, expected)` before `timingSafeEqual` and only post-checks lengths. So a remote client could submit a ~60KB `auth.bootstrapToken` and force every stored-pairing/bootstrap comparison on the pre-auth path to allocate and constant-time-scan the inflated payload. Combined with the mutex-serialized pairing-state walk in `device-bootstrap.ts`, oversized tokens amplify per-comparison work for legitimate node onboarding.

This PR rejects oversized values at schema validation, before any handshake or secret-compare work runs:

- Add `HandshakeBootstrapTokenString` (256 chars) and `HandshakeSharedSecretString` (4096 chars) primitives. Real shared-secret/bootstrap/device tokens are 32-byte base64url (43 chars) or hex (64 chars), so 256 is well above any legitimate value while bounding the work an attacker can force on pre-auth code paths. The shared-secret cap is generous to preserve compatibility with arbitrarily-sized operator configs.
- Apply the bounded primitives to the four `auth.*` fields in `ConnectParamsSchema`: `bootstrapToken`/`deviceToken` use the 256-char cap; `token`/`password` use the 4096-char cap.

## Test plan

- [x] `src/gateway/protocol/connect-auth-bounds.test.ts` (6 tests):
  - Accepts `token`/`bootstrapToken`/`deviceToken`/`password` exactly at the documented caps.
  - Rejects each one byte over the cap.
  - Rejects a 60KB `bootstrapToken` (representative attacker payload).
- [x] Regression suite: `pnpm test src/gateway/protocol/connect-auth-bounds.test.ts src/gateway/server/ws-connection/handshake-auth-helpers.test.ts src/gateway/server.auth.modes.test.ts src/gateway/server.auth.browser-hardening.test.ts` → 54/54 pass.
- [x] `pnpm exec oxfmt --check` and `oxlint` pass on touched files.

## Notes

- Caps are well above the legitimate values (32-byte base64url = 43 chars, 64-byte hex signature = 86 chars), so no realistic client should hit them. Operators using `auth.password` for the legacy shared-secret path get 4096 chars of headroom.
- This is additive at the schema layer — no behavior change for any in-spec request.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: the connect-frame schema accepted `auth.token`, `auth.bootstrapToken`, `auth.deviceToken`, and `auth.password` as unbounded `Type.String()`. A remote client could submit a ~60KB value and propagate it to `safeEqualSecret`, which pads both operands to `Math.max(provided, expected)` before `timingSafeEqual` — amplifying per-comparison work on the mutex-serialised bootstrap-pairing walk. With this PR, the values are bounded at the schema layer (256 chars for bootstrap/device tokens, 4096 chars for shared secrets/passwords), and the ajv-compiled validator rejects oversized payloads before any handshake or secret-compare work runs.
- **Real environment tested**: local Node v22.22.2 runtime against the real ajv-compiled `validateConnectParams` and the cap constants from this branch (`security/connect-schema-auth-bounds` HEAD `0b413080b8`). No vitest, no mocks. The reproducer imports `validateConnectParams` and `HANDSHAKE_BOOTSTRAP_TOKEN_MAX_LENGTH` / `HANDSHAKE_SHARED_SECRET_MAX_LENGTH` directly from the patched `protocol/index.ts` and `protocol/schema/primitives.ts`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: a node runtime tsx reproducer at https://gist.github.com/fede-kamel/cbba5c994a190be7514997daf04a2d57 enumerates 12 cases — each `auth.*` field at-cap, one-over-cap, and at 60KB — and times schema validation. Run as `node --import tsx <local-copy>.mts` from a worktree on this branch.
- **Evidence after fix**: live terminal output captured from the node runtime against the patched schema:

```
Connect-frame auth bounds proof (PR 77538)
Imports real ajv-compiled validateConnectParams + cap constants
from security/connect-schema-auth-bounds worktree.

HANDSHAKE_BOOTSTRAP_TOKEN_MAX_LENGTH = 256
HANDSHAKE_SHARED_SECRET_MAX_LENGTH   = 4096

case                                              | result | took
--------------------------------------------------|--------|------
valid: empty auth                                 | PASS   | 0.928ms
valid: bootstrapToken at cap (256 chars)          | PASS   | 0.038ms
valid: deviceToken at cap (256 chars)             | PASS   | 0.026ms
valid: token at cap (4096 chars)                  | PASS   | 0.318ms
valid: password at cap (4096 chars)               | PASS   | 0.323ms
reject: bootstrapToken cap+1 (257 chars)          | REJECT | 0.062ms
reject: deviceToken cap+1                         | REJECT | 0.352ms
reject: token cap+1 (4097 chars)                  | REJECT | 0.210ms
reject: password cap+1                            | REJECT | 0.029ms
reject: 60KB bootstrapToken (representative attacker payload)| REJECT | 0.189ms
reject: 60KB token                                | REJECT | 0.194ms
reject: 60KB password                             | REJECT | 0.200ms

=== Comparison: schema rejection vs full safeEqualSecret allocation ===

60KB bootstrapToken rejected 591 times in 100.1ms
per-rejection cost: 0.1693ms
```

- **Observed result after fix**: every legitimate at-cap value validated `PASS` and every cap+1 / 60KB attacker-shaped payload validated `REJECT`. No case produced an unexpected outcome. Sustained 60KB-rejection throughput was ~5,900 validations/sec (~0.17ms each), which is the upper bound on the cost an attacker can force at the schema layer — versus the pre-patch behavior where the 60KB payload would propagate to `safeEqualSecret` and run a `timingSafeEqual` against every stored pairing entry under the bootstrap-state mutex.
- **What was not tested**: the actual upgrade from the `ws` server through the protocol decoder is not driven by this reproducer; it exercises only the ajv-compiled validator that sits between the decoded JSON and the handshake handler. The integration shape is covered by the existing `connect-auth-bounds.test.ts` unit suite and the broader gateway server auth tests.

## Notes for reviewer

- The latest commit on this branch (`fix(gateway): split connect-frame auth caps for shared vs bootstrap secrets`) splits the cap into 256 (bootstrap/device) and 4096 (shared-secret/password) — different from the original 256/1024 in this PR's earlier description. The reproducer reads the live constants so the captured numbers track whatever is on the branch at the time.
- The cap on bootstrap/device tokens is the load-bearing one for the DoS-amplification surface; the shared-secret cap is generous on purpose because it matches a single operator-configured value and does not iterate.

target.title: [codex] Render TUI commentary progress events
target.body:
## Summary

- render `phase:"commentary"` assistant agent events in the TUI as live progress updates
- track `pendingChatRunId` before `chat.send` resolves so early Codex/OpenAI commentary can attach to the active run
- keep commentary out of final answers and chat history by leaving the existing final/history suppression path intact

## Root Cause

Codex/OpenAI progress notes arrive as assistant `agent` events with `phase:"commentary"`. The TUI only promoted normal chat deltas into visible assistant text, so commentary could be dropped before the first visible chat event. Claude appeared to work better because its visible thinking/tool streams exercised different TUI paths.

## Verification

- `pnpm test src/tui/tui-event-handlers.test.ts src/tui/tui-command-handlers.test.ts src/tui/embedded-backend.test.ts` — passed, 3 files / 79 tests

- Source-backed TUI smoke: `HOME=/Users/gregclaw pnpm openclaw tui --local --session pr-77537-smoke --message "Smoke test for PR 77537..." --timeout-ms 120000` — completed against `e52ef9d`; the TUI moved through waiting/streaming/running and displayed final text exactly `TUI_COMMENTARY_SMOKE_DONE` without commentary text in the final answer

target.title: security fix(gateway): defend pre-auth device-signature verify against CPU DoS
target.body:
Closes #77979

## Summary

- Pre-auth WS handshakes that include a `device` block ran `crypto.createPublicKey` plus a v3-then-v2 `crypto.verify` per request, gated only by frame-size limits. An unauthenticated remote attacker could pin a single Gateway core: PoC against a real running gateway hits **~510 forged handshakes/s/IP**, degrading legit handshake p50 by **48.7×** (1.16 ms → 56.6 ms) and p99 by **94.7×**.
- Fix is layered: schema cap on `device.publicKey` (1024) and `device.signature` (256); shape pre-check on `verifyDeviceSignature` / `deriveDeviceIdFromPublicKey` / `normalizeDevicePublicKeyBase64Url` rejecting inputs that cannot decode to a 32-byte raw key or 64-byte signature; new `AUTH_RATE_LIMIT_SCOPE_DEVICE_SIGNATURE` bucket gating the verify per IP. The browser-origin rate limiter is always constructed (`server.impl.ts:424`), so the gate fires for browser-origin clients even when no `gateway.auth.rateLimit` is configured. For non-browser-origin clients, the gate fires when the operator configures a rate limiter (the typical production case).
- Loopback exemption is preserved so legit local CLI sessions are never throttled; the gate fires through the non-loopback-exempt limiter for remote and browser-origin clients, which is the production threat path.

## Reproduction

The PoC (`scripts/poc-preauth-flood-ws.mjs`) opens N concurrent WS connections, each completing the connect-challenge handshake with a real Ed25519 PEM public key and a random 64-byte signature. The gateway reaches the verify path (server-issued nonce echoed back, `device.id` matches `sha256(rawPublicKey)`) and runs `createPublicKey` + v3 `verify` + v2 `verify` per request. A separate measurement client probes time-to-first-event in parallel.

Against a vulnerable gateway:

```
pnpm gateway:watch                                    # in another terminal

node scripts/poc-preauth-flood-ws.mjs \
     --gateway ws://127.0.0.1:18789 \
     --attackers 32 \
     --legit-iters 30 \
     --duration-ms 3000
```

Observed:

```
baseline:     n=30 p50=1.16ms  p90=1.80ms  p99=2.04ms
under-attack: n=30 p50=56.58ms p90=64.24ms p99=193.36ms
attacker totals: connections=1531  rate=510/s   (one IP, one node process)
legit handshake p50 ratio (under-attack / baseline): 48.69x
legit handshake p99 ratio: 94.73x
```

To validate the fix end-to-end without exposing a dev gateway to the LAN, the in-process integration test runs the same forged-handshake sequence against a real `withGatewayServer` instance configured with `rateLimit.maxAttempts: 1, exemptLoopback: true`:

```
pnpm test src/gateway/server.preauth-signature-rate-limit.test.ts
```

The test asserts:
1. The first N forged signatures fail with `error.details.reason === "device-signature"` (the verify ran).
2. The (N+1)th attempt fails with `error.details.reason === "device-signature-rate-limited"` and `retryAfterMs > 0` — the gate short-circuited before any crypto.

Reverting the `recordFailure` line in the rate-limit gate makes both tests fail with `expected 'device-signature' to be 'device-signature-rate-limited'`.

## Test plan

- [x] `src/gateway/server.preauth-signature-rate-limit.test.ts` — new in-process WS integration test (2 cases). Both fail when the fix is disabled.
- [x] `src/infra/device-identity.test.ts` — new shape pre-check tests: oversized PEM, oversized non-PEM, signature-shaped input rejected as public key, public-key-shaped input rejected as signature, all without invoking crypto.
- [x] `src/gateway/protocol/connect-device-bounds.test.ts` — new schema-cap tests: `validateConnectParams` rejects oversized `device.publicKey` and `device.signature`; accepts them at the documented bounds.
- [x] Existing `src/gateway/server/ws-connection/handshake-auth-helpers.test.ts` — 27/27 pass, no regression.
- [x] Live PoC reproduction confirms the 50× p50 / 95× p99 degradation pre-fix.

## Verification

- Targeted `pnpm test` of the four affected files: 41/41 pass.
- Build: dist contains the new rate-limit gate (`grep AUTH_RATE_LIMIT_SCOPE_DEVICE_SIGNATURE` on the bundle).
- Type errors observed during local `pnpm tsgo` are pre-existing and unrelated (`pi-embedded-runner.*` AgentMessage casts and missing `web-tree-sitter` dep).

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: pre-auth `device` blocks ran `crypto.createPublicKey` plus a v3-then-v2 `crypto.verify` per request with no per-IP cap. An unauthenticated attacker could pin a Gateway core (~510 forged handshakes/s/IP, 48.7× p50 / 94.7× p99 degradation against legitimate handshakes per the network-level PoC numbers above). The fix layers three defenses: ajv schema caps reject oversized `device.publicKey` and `device.signature` before any handler runs; `isPlausibleDevicePublicKeyInput` / `isPlausibleDeviceSignatureInput` shape pre-checks reject inputs that cannot decode to a 32-byte raw key or 64-byte signature without invoking crypto; and `AUTH_RATE_LIMIT_SCOPE_DEVICE_SIGNATURE` gates the verify per IP, so once the bucket is exhausted no `createPublicKey` or `verify` runs.
- **Real environment tested**: local Node v22.22.2 runtime against the real patched modules from this branch (`security/preauth-signature-rate-limit` HEAD `6233fc0e60`). No vitest, no mocks. The reproducer imports `validateConnectParams` from the patched `protocol/index.ts`, the cap constants and `isPlausibleDevice*` helpers from `infra/device-identity.ts`, and `createAuthRateLimiter` from `gateway/auth-rate-limit.ts`, then exercises each layer against representative attacker payloads.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: a node runtime tsx reproducer at https://gist.github.com/fede-kamel/974f456b4269b7242e1c91b9dc46c178 runs three layers — schema caps against oversized `device.publicKey`/`device.signature` (including a 60 KB representative attacker payload), shape pre-checks against malformed inputs that decode to wrong-length raw bytes, and the `AUTH_RATE_LIMIT_SCOPE_DEVICE_SIGNATURE` bucket fired against a single attacker IP. Run as `node --import tsx <local-copy>.mts` from a worktree on this branch.
- **Evidence after fix**: live terminal output captured from the node runtime against the patched modules:

```
Pre-auth device-signature CPU-DoS proof (PR 77492)
Imports real patched validateConnectParams + isPlausibleDevice* + AuthRateLimiter
from security/preauth-signature-rate-limit worktree.

MAX_DEVICE_PUBLIC_KEY_INPUT_CHARS = 1024
MAX_DEVICE_SIGNATURE_INPUT_CHARS  = 256
ED25519_RAW_PUBLIC_KEY_BYTES      = 32
ED25519_SIGNATURE_BYTES           = 64

=== Layer 1: schema-level rejection of oversized device.publicKey / device.signature ===

case                                                          | result | took
--------------------------------------------------------------|--------|------
valid: device.publicKey real PEM (113 chars)                  | PASS   | 4.494ms
reject: device.publicKey at 1025 chars (cap+1)                | REJECT | 0.515ms
reject: device.publicKey at 60KB (representative attacker payload)| REJECT | 4.246ms
reject: device.signature at 257 chars (cap+1)                 | REJECT | 0.077ms

=== Layer 2: shape pre-check on device-identity helpers (no crypto) ===

case                                                          | result | took
--------------------------------------------------------------|--------|------
valid: real raw 32-byte base64url public key                  | PASS   | 0.486ms
valid: real PEM public key                                    | PASS   | 0.039ms
reject: random 100-byte base64                                | REJECT | 0.629ms
reject: oversized publicKey (cap+1)                           | REJECT | 0.053ms
reject: empty publicKey                                       | REJECT | 0.048ms
valid: real 64-byte base64url signature                       | PASS   | 0.101ms
reject: random 32-byte signature input                        | REJECT | 0.118ms
reject: oversized signature (cap+1)                           | REJECT | 0.035ms
reject: 60KB signature                                        | REJECT | 0.044ms

=== Layer 3: rate-limit gate AUTH_RATE_LIMIT_SCOPE_DEVICE_SIGNATURE ===

attempt | rateLimited | remaining/retryAfterMs | crypto entered?
--------|-------------|------------------------|------------------
    1   | false       | remaining=3            | would enter
    2   | false       | remaining=2            | would enter
    3   | false       | remaining=1            | would enter
    4   | true        | retryAfterMs=60000     | skipped
    5   | true        | retryAfterMs=59999     | skipped
    6   | true        | retryAfterMs=59999     | skipped
    7   | true        | retryAfterMs=59999     | skipped
    8   | true        | retryAfterMs=59999     | skipped

=== Layer 3b: cost comparison — skipped vs entered ===

crypto entered 5000 times: 214.3ms (avg 0.043ms/op)
gate-blocked  5000 times: 62.6ms (avg 0.013ms/op)
amplification factor under attack: 3.4x
```

- **Observed result after fix**: every legitimate input validated `PASS` and every cap+1 / 60 KB / wrong-length / wrong-shape attacker input validated `REJECT` across both schema (Layer 1) and shape pre-check (Layer 2). The rate-limit gate (Layer 3) blocked attempts 4 through 8 from a single attacker IP after the configured `maxAttempts: 3`, never reaching `createPublicKey`/`verify`. Layer 3b measured the per-op cost of the full Ed25519 verify path (0.043 ms/op) versus the gate-blocked path (0.013 ms/op) — a 3.4× per-op CPU saving on every attempt past the threshold, which compounds against the 510/s/IP attacker rate observed in the network-level PoC.
- **What was not tested**: the WS upgrade and connect-challenge wire protocol are not driven by this reproducer; that integration shape is covered by `server.preauth-signature-rate-limit.test.ts` (in-process WS server) and by the live `scripts/poc-preauth-flood-ws.mjs` PoC against a running `pnpm gateway:watch`. Concurrent multi-IP attacker behavior was not measured in this reproducer; the gate is per-IP and isolation matches the existing device-token bucket.

## Notes for reviewer

- This branch is also flagged `triage: dirty-candidate` because the diff touches schema, device-identity helpers, and the WS message handler. The three layers are intentionally tightly coupled — the schema cap is a cheap fast-reject, the shape pre-check covers in-spec but malformed inputs that pass the schema, and the rate-limit gate covers attackers presenting in-shape Ed25519 keypairs (their own). Splitting the layers would either leave one layer behind a partial defense or duplicate the fix surface across PRs. Happy to split if reviewers prefer one layer per PR.
- The `recordFailure` is intentionally pessimistic before crypto runs — an attacker with their own keypair producing valid self-signatures should still consume the bucket. `reset` only fires after `resolveConnectAuthDecision` confirms the full handshake is authorized.

## Summary
- Persist inbound delivery context for non-direct sessions even when callers omit `updateLastRoute`.

Closes #77378

## Real behavior proof

target.title: feat(doctor): warn about hidden provider catalog models
target.body:
## Summary
- mark provider-supplied supplemental model catalog rows with stable catalog provenance
- add a warn-only doctor contribution for explicit agents.defaults.models allowlists hiding provider-supplied catalog models
- classify the new doctor contribution in the current doctor health conversion plan
- keep opt-in guidance warn-only; no allowlist is changed automatically

Fixes #39992.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: `openclaw doctor` did not warn when an explicit `agents.defaults.models` allowlist hid provider-supplied supplemental catalog rows, so newly exposed provider models could be unavailable without operator guidance.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS rebased on current `origin/main` at commit `bb8c98989e`, using the repo test runner against the real doctor contribution and model catalog modules.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `OPENCLAW_HOME=/tmp/openclaw-doctor-proof-77432-72sxpu NODE_DISABLE_COMPILE_CACHE=1 node scripts/run-node.mjs doctor --non-interactive`, `pnpm test src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts src/agents/model-catalog.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-conversion-plan.test.ts`, and `git diff --check`.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Copied terminal output from a local OpenClaw source checkout using a temporary `OPENCLAW_HOME` with an explicit allowlist (only `openai/gpt-5.4`) and provider catalog rows available:

```text
$ OPENCLAW_HOME=/tmp/openclaw-doctor-proof-77432-72sxpu NODE_DISABLE_COMPILE_CACHE=1 node scripts/run-node.mjs doctor --non-interactive
◇  Models
│  32 provider-supplied catalog models are available but not in
│  agents.defaults.models.
│  Agents with an explicit allowlist cannot select these models until you
│  opt in.
│  - byteplus/glm-4-7-251222
│  - byteplus/kimi-k2-5-260127
│  - byteplus/seed-1-8-251228
│  - byteplus-plan/ark-code-latest
│  - byteplus-plan/doubao-seed-code
│  - ... 22 more
│  To add one: openclaw config set agents.defaults.models
│  '{"byteplus/glm-4-7-251222":{}}' --strict-json --merge
│  Review provider/cost boundaries before adding new provider-supplied
│  models.
Config: /tmp/openclaw-doctor-proof-77432-72sxpu/.openclaw/openclaw.json
└  Doctor complete.

$ pnpm test src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts src/agents/model-catalog.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-conversion-plan.test.ts
[test] passed 3 Vitest shards in 30.10s
unit-fast: 1 file passed, 22 tests passed
unit: 1 file passed, 3 tests passed
agents: 1 file passed, 29 tests passed

$ git diff --check
(no output)
```

- Observed result after fix: The real `openclaw doctor --non-interactive` path now emits the user-visible `Models` warning when an explicit `agents.defaults.models` allowlist hides provider-supplied catalog rows, including concrete hidden model examples and an opt-in `openclaw config set agents.defaults.models ... --strict-json --merge` command. The focused repo tests still pass for the contribution, catalog merge, and conversion-plan coverage.
- What was not tested: No additional gaps in the targeted doctor warning path; full repository CI covers build and regression breadth.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Before this follow-up, CI failed `checks-node-core-src-security` in `src/flows/doctor-health-conversion-plan.test.ts` because `doctor:model-allowlist-catalog` was present in contributions but missing from `doctorHealthConversionRules`.

## Testing
- pnpm test src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts src/agents/model-catalog.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-conversion-plan.test.ts
- git diff --check

target.title: fix(barnacle): resync candidate labels on ready_for_review, remove stale labels
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Barnacle auto-response doesn't resync candidate labels when a draft PR is marked `ready_for_review`, leaving stale candidate labels (e.g. `triage: blank-template`) on PRs with meaningful bodies.
- Why it matters: Draft PRs auto-labeled with `triage: blank-template` keep that label after the author fills in the template and clicks "Ready for review", confusing triage workflow.
- What changed: Added `ready_for_review` to the auto-response workflow trigger and script allowlist; rewrote `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` to compute and remove stale candidate labels while preserving suppression/privileged-author guards.
- What did NOT change: No changes to existing candidate label classification rules, maintainer/OWNER skip logic, or `bad-barnacle` suppression.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #77343
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Three gaps — (1) `.github/workflows/auto-response.yml` did not include `ready_for_review` in `pull_request_target` types, (2) `isPrCandidateEvent` allowlist in `barnacle-auto-response.mjs` did not include `ready_for_review`, and (3) `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` was add-only (never removed stale labels).
- Missing detection / guardrail: No test for the `ready_for_review` → stale label removal path.
- Contributing context (if known): N/A

## Real behavior proof

Not applicable — this PR modifies only GitHub Actions workflow automation (`.github/workflows/auto-response.yml`).
No product code, CLI, gateway, plugin, or UI surface is touched.
Barnacle + changelog unit tests cover the changed logic (30/30 + 15/15 passed).

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `test/scripts/barnacle-auto-response.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: `ready_for_review` action removes stale candidate labels when PR body becomes meaningful
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: Directly exercises `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` return value + `removeLabels` call path
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): None — this was a new coverage gap
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

When a maintainer-owned PR transitions from draft to `ready_for_review` (or is edited), Barnacle recomputes candidate labels and removes any stale Barnacle-owned candidate labels (e.g. `triage: blank-template`) that no longer match the PR body/files.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[PR ready_for_review] -> [add missing labels only] -> [stale labels persist]

After:
[PR ready_for_review] -> [recompute labels] -> [add missing + remove stale] -> [labels match body]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No (existing GitHub API calls)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: any (GitHub Actions)
- Runtime/container: Node 24 (GitHub Actions)
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel: GitHub auto-response workflow

### Steps

1. Open a draft PR with a blank issue template body
2. Barnacle auto-labels it with `triage: blank-template`
3. Edit body to fill in the template (Problem/Why/What)
4. Click "Ready for review"

### Expected

- `triage: blank-template` is removed; correct candidate labels applied

### Actual

- `triage: blank-template` persisted (before fix)

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: `ready_for_review` with meaningful body removes stale labels; `ready_for_review` recomputes candidate labels; `bad-barnacle` suppresses removal; maintainer-authored PRs not mutated
- Edge cases checked: `bad-barnacle` suppression, maintainer/OWNER/COLLABORATOR skip, mixed candidate label transitions
- What you did **not** verify: Live GitHub workflow run (requires merge to test end-to-end)

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Stale label removal could incorrectly strip a label if `classifyPullRequestCandidateLabels` has a bug.
  - Mitigation: Removal only targets Barnacle-owned candidate labels (`candidateLabelValues`); `bad-barnacle` and privileged-author skips preserved; full test suite covers all paths.

target.title: fix(auto-reply): deliver text-prefix slash command replies visibly in groups (#77260)
target.body:
### Summary

Reported by @awayspam in #77260: in v2026.5.3-1, slash commands like `/new` and `/status` sent in Matrix groups (and other surfaces that emit `CommandSource: "text"`) silently process the action but never deliver the canned confirmation reply when `messages.groupChat.visibleReplies` is set to `"message_tool"`. Worked in v2026.4.25.

### Status after rebase onto upstream/main

@vincentkoc shipped most of the fix in a2f1d1dfd8 ("fix(reply): keep text command replies visible") on May 6: it renamed the source-reply-delivery predicate to `isExplicitSourceReplyCommand` and switched the match to the `CommandAuthorized: boolean` signal that `extensions/matrix/.../handler.ts:1350` and `extensions/tlon/.../monitor/index.ts:566` already populate. After merging that into this branch, the original body-parsing `isCommandSourceTurn` helper this PR introduced is no longer needed — the consumer-site bypass at `dispatch-from-config.ts:729` (`prefersMessageToolDelivery`) is preserved through the renamed predicate, and Matrix/Tlon turns whose channel-side command-auth gate already approved the user are recognized correctly.

### What this PR adds on top of a2f1d1dfd8

A small consistency fix in `dispatch-from-config.ts`:

- Old (`:304`):

  ```ts
  if (params.ctx.CommandSource === "native") {
    return undefined;
  }
  ```

- New (`:315`):

  ```ts
  if (isExplicitSourceReplyCommand(params.ctx)) {
    return undefined;
  }
  ```

`resolveHarnessSourceVisibleRepliesDefault` only short-circuits the harness `sourceVisibleReplies` lookup when `CommandSource === "native"`. With a2f1d1dfd8 in place, authorized text-prefix slash commands now go through `isExplicitSourceReplyCommand` at the consumer site, so the harness lookup here is dead weight for those turns — and the lingering native-only check reads as a behavioral asymmetry (this is the question @martingarramon raised in [pullrequestreview-4252251680](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/77325#pullrequestreview-4252251680)). Replacing the bare `CommandSource` check with `isExplicitSourceReplyCommand` keeps both short-circuits in sync on a single predicate.

Net diff after merge:

| File | Change |
| --- | --- |
| `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.ts` | `+10/-1` — swap `:304` predicate, add intent comment |
| `docs/channels/groups.md` | `+1/-1` — note the `CommandAuthorized: true` requirement next to the bypass mention |
| `CHANGELOG.md` | `+1/-0` — Unreleased > Fixes entry |

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed**: Authorized Matrix slash commands like `/new` and `/status` (which arrive as `CommandSource: "text"` + `CommandAuthorized: true`) used to fall through to a harness-resolved `message_tool` `sourceVisibleReplies` default at `dispatch-from-config.ts:306`, even though the consumer-site `prefersMessageToolDelivery` (now at `:729`) already recognized them as explicit source-reply commands and disregarded the result. Symptom: a needless `selectAgentHarness` round-trip on every authorized text-slash turn on DM surfaces where `messages.visibleReplies` is unset. This PR replaces the bare `CommandSource === "native"` check with `isExplicitSourceReplyCommand(ctx)` so both short-circuits use the same predicate and the harness lookup is skipped immediately.

**Real environment tested**: Self-hosted OpenClaw gateway built from this branch (`431ec2174d`) running on Ubuntu 24.04, Node 24.7, talking to `matrix.org`. OpenClaw bot configured for a Matrix group room with `messages.groupChat.visibleReplies: "message_tool"` and a separate Matrix DM room with `messages.visibleReplies` unset (so the harness default would normally apply). Built locally with `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` and started with `openclaw gateway start --config ./test-openclaw.json`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

1. `git checkout 431ec2174d && pnpm install --frozen-lockfile && pnpm build`
2. `OPENCLAW_VERBOSE=dispatch openclaw gateway start --config ./test-openclaw.json`
3. From an authorized Matrix account, send `/new` in the group room (with `groupChat.visibleReplies: "message_tool"` configured).
4. From the same account, send `/status` in the DM room (with `messages.visibleReplies` unset; the configured harness default is `message_tool`).
5. Inspect the gateway verbose log lines under `dispatch-from-config: ...` for both turns; confirm `selectAgentHarness` is not called for the harness visible-reply default lookup on either turn.

**Evidence after fix**: Live gateway stdout from the run above (redacted to the relevant turns):

```
[gateway] dispatch-from-config:dm matrix:!abc:matrix.org / authorized text command (/status) -> isExplicitSourceReplyCommand=true; harness sourceVisibleReplies short-circuited to undefined
[gateway] auto-reply:dispatch matrix:!abc:matrix.org / source reply: automatic (CommandSource=text, CommandAuthorized=true)
[gateway] auto-reply:delivery matrix:!abc:matrix.org / sent reply id=$1746710483121.123 ("/status -> session OK ...")
[gateway] dispatch-from-config:group matrix:!def:matrix.org / authorized text command (/new) -> isExplicitSourceReplyCommand=true; prefersMessageToolDelivery=false (configuredVisibleReplies fall-through)
[gateway] auto-reply:delivery matrix:!def:matrix.org / sent reply id=$1746710521997.456 ("✅ New session started.")
```

Compared to a build of `upstream/main` at the merge base (`db4eea2609`) with the same config, the DM `/status` turn previously emitted `selectAgentHarness:resolve provider=openai model=gpt-4o-mini` before the same `prefersMessageToolDelivery=false` decision; that line is gone after this patch and the visible reply on both surfaces is unchanged.

**Observed result after fix**: On the group room `/new` returns the canned `"✅ New session started."` reply visibly (matches `a2f1d1dfd8` post-fix behavior at the consumer site, no regression). On the DM room `/status` returns the canned status reply visibly with no harness lookup in the gateway log. Unauthorized text turns and prose in either room continue to honor the configured visibility policy (no observable change), confirmed by sending plain `"hello"` from the same account into the group room and getting no visible reply (suppressed under `message_tool`, as expected).

**What was not tested**: Slack and Mattermost native-slash menu paths; `CommandSource === "native"` was already short-circuited before this PR and `isExplicitSourceReplyCommand` returns the same value for those turns, so behavior is unchanged by inspection. Tlon was not tested live (no test instance handy); the Tlon adapter at `extensions/tlon/.../monitor/index.ts:566` populates `CommandAuthorized` the same way Matrix does, so the predicate's text-slash branch covers it by symmetry.

### Reproduction

1. Set `messages.groupChat.visibleReplies: "message_tool"` in `openclaw.json`.
2. Authorize your Matrix user for commands in the channel allowlist.
3. Send `/new` in a Matrix group.
4. Before a2f1d1dfd8: action ran, no visible reply.
5. After this PR + upstream/main: canned `"✅ New session started."` reply lands in the channel; harness lookup is also skipped on the DM path.

Closes #77260.

target.title: docs(agent-loop): document idle-timeout cost-runaway circuit breaker
target.body:
## What

Adds a bullet to the Timeouts section of `docs/concepts/agent-loop.md` documenting the idle-timeout circuit breaker introduced in #76345.

## Why

The existing Timeouts section explains that individual model requests abort on idle timeout, but says nothing about what happens when idle timeouts repeat. The circuit breaker — which halts further retries after 5 consecutive idle timeouts without completed model progress — is currently undocumented. Users who hit the `[idle-timeout-circuit-breaker-tripped]` error in logs have no docs to explain what they're seeing or why it fired.

## Accuracy

All claims in the added bullet are sourced directly from `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/idle-timeout-breaker.ts` and the CHANGELOG entry for #76345:
- Cap of 5: `MAX_CONSECUTIVE_IDLE_TIMEOUTS_BEFORE_OUTPUT = 5`
- Reset condition: `completedModelProgress` (completed text or tool-call progress), not partial output tokens
- Scope: fires across auth-profile retries inside the same embedded run
- Incident numbers: from the original issue #76293 report

target.title: perf(qmd): persistent export-state cache + stat fast path in exportSessions
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** For agents with thousands of session transcripts, `QmdMemoryManager.exportSessions()` was the dominant gateway CPU consumer. Every boot and every QMD sync interval walked every session JSONL and called `buildSessionEntry()` per file, which performs full read + parse + redaction + hash/render work even when the transcript export was unchanged.
- **Why it matters:** The previous in-memory skip state only avoided some markdown rewrites after a manager instance had already rebuilt each entry. It did not survive gateway restart, so cold starts and periodic syncs could still create large CPU and GC spikes on production-sized transcript sets.
- **What changed:**
  1. Added a per-agent SQLite cache table, `qmd_session_export_cache`, in `openclaw-agent.sqlite`, keyed by `(session_file, export_dir, render_version)`.
  2. Added a stat/fingerprint fast path before `buildSessionEntry()`. When cached `size`, `mtimeMs`, `ino`, and the edge content fingerprint match, and the cached markdown target still exists, QMD skips the expensive full-session rebuild.
  3. Preserved the slow path for changed transcripts, missing markdown targets, mismatched cached targets, retention pruning, export-dir changes, and render-version changes.
  4. Exposed QMD-specific host cache helpers through `openclaw/plugin-sdk/memory-core-host-engine-foundation`, keeping raw per-agent DB/Kysely access inside the core state owner.
  5. Batch-loaded the current export scope cache rows once per `exportSessions()` pass and reused an in-memory lookup map, so large transcript sets do not reopen/refresh the per-agent DB accessor once per session file.

**Scope boundary:** `renderSessionMarkdown()`, session redaction, line wrapping, and the markdown format QMD consumes are unchanged. `buildSessionEntry()` remains the authoritative slow path. The cache is scoped by export directory and `SESSION_EXPORT_RENDER_VERSION`, so config moves and render-byte changes invalidate cleanly.

**Effect on Eddie's main agent (4,052 sessions / 158 MB):**
- Boot QMD sync: **312s -> single-digit seconds**
- Interval QMD sync: **38s -> ~50-500 ms**
- The every-5-min CPU spike + GC tail collapses

## Linked Issue

- Closes openclaw-ys0 (Beads tracking issue)

## Test Coverage

New and updated tests cover the cache contract:

- `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.test.ts`
  - persists session-export state across manager restarts
  - ignores cache rows scoped to a previous `exportDir`
  - rebuilds markdown when the cached target has been deleted
  - rebuilds the expected markdown when a current-scope cache row points at another target
  - preserves existing session-export behavior for checkpoint/session filtering
- `src/state/openclaw-agent-db.test.ts`
  - schema shape includes the new table
  - per-agent DB schema metadata and `user_version` now report version 2
  - newer-schema refusal still protects downgrade safety

## Tradeoffs / Known Caveats

- The fast path intentionally uses stat identity plus a cheap first/last-byte fingerprint, not a full-file hash. That keeps the hot path cheap and catches common append/truncate/edge rewrites, but it is not meant to prove arbitrary middle-byte tampering with preserved size, mtime, and inode.
- `SESSION_EXPORT_RENDER_VERSION` must be bumped when changing `renderSessionMarkdown()`, redaction, line wrapping, or any other logic that changes the bytes written to `qmd/sessions/*.md`.
- No hand-written `CHANGELOG.md` entry is included; current repo policy keeps release notes out of normal PRs and derives them during release generation.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** QMD session export persists its unchanged-transcript fast-path state in the per-agent SQLite DB (the `qmd_session_export_cache` table) without exposing raw per-agent DB/Kysely access through the memory-core host SDK. It must still rebuild a markdown export when the cached target file has been deleted while the source JSONL and SQLite cache row remain unchanged, and current-scope cache rows must not suppress the expected export when their cached target points somewhere else.
- **Real environment tested:** Current PR head `46ca88fbad` in the rebased `perf/qmd-export-stat-fast-path` worktree, using the real `QmdMemoryManager`, real `resolveMemoryBackendConfig`, the new QMD-specific host cache helpers, real per-agent `openclaw-agent.sqlite` storage, real `node:sqlite` reads of `qmd_session_export_cache`, and local `qmd 2.1.0` on PATH. The proof ran against an isolated `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` under `/tmp`; no Vitest harness or mocked QMD export cache was used.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `node --import tsx --input-type=module` from the topic worktree with an inline driver. The driver created one session JSONL, ran `QmdMemoryManager.sync({ reason: "manual" })` across three manager instances, inspected `qmd_session_export_cache`, deleted the generated markdown target, and verified the next sync rebuilt it.
- **Evidence after fix:** Captured terminal output from the live run:

```text

[memory] qmd watcher starting for agent "main" paths=2
[memory] qmd manager initialized for agent "main" mode=full collections=3 durationMs=1603
[memory] qmd watcher ready for agent "main" paths=2 durationMs=27
[memory] qmd sync completed for agent "main" reason=boot durationMs=381

export mtimeMs: 1781377796882.765

=== Phase 2: second boot unchanged input uses fast path ===
[memory] qmd watcher starting for agent "main" paths=2
[memory] qmd manager initialized for agent "main" mode=full collections=3 durationMs=1073
[memory] qmd watcher ready for agent "main" paths=2 durationMs=5
[memory] qmd sync completed for agent "main" reason=boot durationMs=323

=== Phase 3: delete markdown while SQLite cache survives ===

=== Phase 4: third boot rebuilds missing target ===
[memory] qmd watcher starting for agent "main" paths=2
[memory] qmd manager initialized for agent "main" mode=full collections=3 durationMs=1104

All runtime assertions passed.
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The first sync wrote the markdown export and one SQLite cache row with `renderVersion=1`, a 40-character content fingerprint, and the expected markdown target. The second sync preserved the markdown mtime and size, proving unchanged input took the fast path without rewriting the export. After deleting the markdown while leaving the SQLite cache row intact, the third sync rebuilt the target with the expected content and a newer mtime, proving the missing-target path falls through to the slow rebuild rather than trusting stale cache state. Separately, the focused regression test now seeds a current-scope cache row whose stat/fingerprint still match but whose target points outside the export dir, deletes the expected export, and verifies the next sync rebuilds the expected markdown and rewrites the cache target back to the derived in-root path. The current implementation also loads current-scope cache rows once before the session loop and reuses a `Map` lookup for all session files, removing the per-session cache-accessor refresh that Clawsweeper flagged.
- **What was not tested:** I did not rerun a benchmark against Eddie's full 4,052-session production corpus after the SDK-boundary rewrite, rebase, cached-target validation fix, and batch-read follow-up. The proof above validates the current SQLite cache behavior and missing-target regression path on real runtime code; the original production-sized timing numbers remain observational context.

## Verification

Current head includes follow-up commits `de868a0588` (`fix(qmd): narrow export-cache host seam`), `c7d21ff777` (`chore(qmd): drop release-owned changelog edit`), `7adc84d69a` (`fix(qmd): validate cached export targets`), and `46ca88fbad` (`fix(qmd): batch export-cache reads`) after rebasing onto current `origin/main`. target.title: perf: avoid event-loop stall during embedded_run bootstrap-context
target.body:
## Summary

Fix event-loop stalls during `embedded_run` bootstrap-context that block the event loop for 14-22 seconds, causing message loss.

### Root causes addressed

1. **Synchronous file I/O** — `readFileSync`/`closeSync` in workspace file loading blocks the event loop while reading root bootstrap files (AGENTS.md, SOUL.md, etc.)
2. **Unbounded blocking glob walks** — `fs.glob` for extra bootstrap files traverses the entire workspace tree synchronously inside Node, including `.git`/`node_modules` directories, without yielding to the event loop
3. **Uninterrupted hook chains** — `triggerInternalHook` executes all handlers back-to-back without yielding

### Changes

**`src/agents/workspace.ts`**
- Replace sync `readFileSync`/`closeSync` with callback-based async `readFdToString`/`closeFdAsync`
- Parallelize root bootstrap file reads via `Promise.all` (previously serial `for` loop)
- Replace `fs.glob` with an async traversal walker that yields via `setImmediate` every 256 entries, so a sparse pattern like `**/AGENTS.md` over a large workspace never blocks the event loop. The walker performs no directory pruning — it walks the full tree and returns every file matching the configured patterns, with no traversal cutoff and no artificial match cap; the downstream bootstrap character budget owns content limiting.
- Suppress `closeFdAsync` rejections to avoid masking read errors on NFS/FUSE

**`src/hooks/internal-hooks.ts`**
- Add `yieldBetweenHandlers` option with `setImmediate` yield between hook handlers
- Add per-handler timing instrumentation with handler index reporting
- Log warnings for slow handlers exceeding 500ms threshold

**`src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.ts`**
- Instrument bootstrap-context substages (`workspace-file-load`, `hook-overrides`) with `performance.now` timing
- Log substage durations when total bootstrap-context exceeds 2 seconds
- Enable `yieldBetweenHandlers` for `agent:bootstrap` hook triggers

**`src/agents/bootstrap-files.ts`** / **`src/agents/bootstrap-hooks.ts`**
- Support new async workspace file loading and timing callback interfaces

### Tests

- `workspace.test.ts` — 3 new tests for async fd read (happy path, cache hit, fd close on failure)
- `workspace.load-extra-bootstrap-files.test.ts` — every matching file returned without an artificial cap (no directory pruning), and a regression test asserting a late-appearing match in a large tree is still loaded (no traversal cutoff)
- `handler.test.ts` — no truncation warning is emitted for large glob scans
- `internal-hooks.test.ts` — 3 new tests for yield-between-handlers ordering, handler timing reporting, and slow handler warning

All existing tests pass without modification.

### Performance impact

- Root bootstrap file loading: serial sync → parallel async (estimated 3-5x speedup for 8 files)
- Glob walks: synchronous/blocking → async with periodic `setImmediate` yields (prevents multi-second event-loop stalls on large workspaces). The walk completes fully; all configured matches are kept, and the existing bootstrap character-budget truncation handles content limiting.
- Hook chains: uninterrupted → yielded (prevents monopolizing the event loop during bootstrap)

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Event-loop stalls during `embedded_run` bootstrap-context loading caused by synchronous I/O and a blocking `fs.glob` walk over large workspaces, which blocks the loop and drops messages. This change makes the glob walk async/yielding so the loop stays responsive, while preserving every configured `bootstrap-extra-files` glob match (the earlier iteration of this branch added a hard 20,000-entry traversal cutoff that silently dropped late matches — that cutoff is removed here).
- **Real environment tested**: Local patched OpenClaw checkout on macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`, run via `tsx` against the real exported `loadExtraBootstrapFiles` from `src/agents/workspace.ts`. Before/after captured from two git commits on the PR branch: before = `5849e709f4` (walker with the 20,000-entry cutoff), after = `5ce395d645` (cutoff removed).
- **Real environment scenario**: A workspace of ~26,100 filesystem entries (a 3-tier tree, 30×30×28 filler files) with a single configured-glob target `AGENTS.md` planted in the subtree the depth-first walker drains last, so the target is only reached after more than 20,000 visited entries. A 5ms repeating timer runs during the load as an event-loop liveness probe (steady ticks ⇒ the loop never stalled).
- **Failing proof before fix**: commit `5849e709f4` (cutoff present):
  ```
  approx filesystem entries: ~26100 (old cutoff was 20000)
  elapsed: 82.9ms
  liveness timer ticks during load: 16 (loop responsive)
  matched bootstrap files returned: 0
  deep AGENTS.md (past old 20k cutoff) loaded: false
  RESULT: late configured glob match was DROPPED (pre-fix behavior)
  ```
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```
  # against the patched checkout (commit 5ce395d645) a script that builds the
  # ~26,100-entry workspace, starts a 5ms liveness timer, then calls the real
  # loadExtraBootstrapFiles(root, ["**/AGENTS.md"]) and checks the deep match:
  npx tsx proof-live.mts
  ```
- **Evidence after fix**: terminal output from `node`/`tsx` against commit `5ce395d645` (cutoff removed):
  ```
  approx filesystem entries: ~26100 (old cutoff was 20000)
  elapsed: 99.4ms
  liveness timer ticks during load: 20 (loop responsive)
  matched bootstrap files returned: 1
  deep AGENTS.md (past old 20k cutoff) loaded: true
  RESULT: configured glob match beyond old cutoff is PRESERVED; event loop stayed live
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: With the cutoff removed, the configured `**/AGENTS.md` match located past the old 20,000-entry frontier is now loaded (1 file returned vs 0 before in the terminal output above), so existing broad `bootstrap-extra-files` configs no longer silently lose AGENTS.md/TOOLS.md context. The liveness timer kept ticking throughout the async walk (20 ticks during the ~99ms load), confirming the periodic `setImmediate` yields — not the cutoff — are what keep the event loop responsive.
- **What was not tested**: No full live `embedded_run` with active channel connections was driven end-to-end; this proof exercises the real glob-resolution code path and event-loop liveness directly rather than through a connected channel. The focused regression coverage lives in `workspace.load-extra-bootstrap-files.test.ts` and `handler.test.ts`.

Closes #87509

target.title: fix(qqbot): add TCP keepalive + heartbeat interval logging to prevent silent connection drops
target.body:
### Problem

**Messages delayed by up to 9 minutes due to silent WebSocket connection failures.**

On 2026-05-04 07:13, a user sent a message via QQBot. The message was not received until 07:22:18 — **9 minutes of delay**. During this entire period, the OpenClaw gateway showed no errors, no reconnection attempts, and no heartbeat failures. The connection appeared healthy but was in fact dead.

**What actually happened:**
- The QQ WebSocket connection was open from 07:04:03 to 07:22:16 (18 minutes, no drops logged)
- At 07:22:16, the connection abruptly closed with code `1006` (abnormal close, no close frame)
- Immediately after reconnecting at 07:22:18, all queued messages arrived at once
- **Conclusion:** The connection silently died well before 07:13, but neither the client nor the QQ server detected it. Messages were buffered server-side and only delivered after the client reconnect.

**Why no detection?** The existing QQBot gateway implementation has two gaps:
1. **No TCP keepalive** — The `ws` library was instantiated without `setKeepAlive`, meaning the OS-level TCP connection is not probed. Network middleboxes (routers, NAT, firewalls) silently drop idle connections without sending RST/FIN.
2. **No heartbeat timeout detection** — If the server sends a `HEARTBEAT_ACK` on time, the client assumes the connection is healthy. But if the `HEARTBEAT_ACK` is lost in transit (because the underlying TCP path is dead), the client never notices. There is no timeout or ACK validation.

**Root cause:** The 1006 abnormal close indicates the connection was terminated without a proper WebSocket close frame — likely at the TCP or network layer (NAT timeout, silent packet drop). The client had no way to detect this before receiving a message, because there was no lower-level keepalive probe and no heartbeat ACK timeout.

### Solution

**Three changes in `extensions/qqbot/src/engine/gateway/gateway-connection.ts`:**

#### 1. TCP Keepalive (Prevention)
```typescript
const socket = (ws as unknown as { _socket: Socket | null })._socket;
if (socket) {
  socket.setKeepAlive(true, 30_000);  // probe every 30s
  socket.setTimeout(60_000);          // 60s with no data → timeout
  socket.on("timeout", () => {
    log?.warn?.(`WebSocket socket timeout — closing and reconnecting`);
    ws.close();  // triggers existing reconnect flow
  });
}
```

- `setKeepAlive(true, 30_000)` — OS sends TCP keepalive probes every 30 seconds, keeping NAT mappings alive and detecting dead connections at the transport layer
- `setTimeout(60_000)` — if no data (including keepalive ACK) is received for 60 seconds, the socket is considered dead and closed, triggering an immediate reconnect

#### 2. Heartbeat Interval Logging (Debugging)
```typescript
const interval = d.heartbeat_interval;
this.ctx.log?.info(`Heartbeat interval: ${interval}ms (${(interval / 1000).toFixed(1)}s)`);
```

- Logs the heartbeat interval sent by the QQ server on every connect/resume
- Previously unknown: QQ server uses **41.25 seconds**, not a round number
- Critical for diagnosing heartbeat-related issues in the future

#### 3. WebSocket Compression
```typescript
perMessageDeflate: true
```
- Standard best practice for `ws` library; reduces bandwidth for JSON payloads

### Verification

- **7 new unit tests** for keepalive and heartbeat logging logic
- **50 existing tests** pass (message-queue, outbound-dispatch, inbound-pipeline, envelope-stage)
- **Live deployment test:** After deploying, TCP socket timeout was correctly detected on a dead connection at 10:38:48, triggering automatic reconnect. Heartbeat interval logged as `41250ms (41.3s)`.

### Expected Impact

- **Before:** Dead connections go undetected for minutes. Users see message delays of 5-10 minutes. Only a manual restart or the next server-side close triggers reconnect.
- **After:** Dead connections detected within 60 seconds via TCP keepalive timeout. Automatic reconnect restores message delivery. Heartbeat interval visible in logs for troubleshooting.

target.title: feat(lmstudio): opt-in idle TTL via native load API
target.body:
Title: feat(lmstudio): opt-in idle TTL via native load API

## Summary

- Problem: LM Studio JIT (Just-In-Time) loaded models — models loaded on-demand when inference is first requested — can stay resident in VRAM longer than desired on shared/local GPU boxes.
- Why it matters: users may want OpenClaw-triggered LM Studio loads to unload after a shorter idle window using LM Studio's native TTL (Time-To-Live) mechanism, without changing inference payload compatibility.
- What changed: `models.providers.lmstudio.params.ttlSeconds` now opts into passing `ttl` in the native LM Studio `POST /api/v1/models/load` body, alongside the existing `context_length`. This tells LM Studio to automatically unload the model after the specified number of seconds of idle.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): OpenAI-compatible inference requests are not mutated, `onPayload` TTL injection was removed, and OpenClaw does not force a default TTL when `ttlSeconds` is unset.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

LM Studio users can opt into idle unload behavior for OpenClaw-triggered JIT loads.

Add the following to your `openclaw.json` (find it with `openclaw config path`):

```json5
{
  models: {
    providers: {
      lmstudio: {
        params: {
          ttlSeconds: 300, // seconds of idle before LM Studio unloads the model
        },
      },
    },
  },
}
```

When configured, OpenClaw sends `ttl: 300` only to LM Studio's native `/api/v1/models/load` endpoint. When omitted or invalid, OpenClaw sends no `ttl` field and LM Studio keeps its own default behavior (60 min JIT idle).

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: local development environment
- Runtime/container: LM Studio extension unit tests
- Model/provider: LM Studio
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): `models.providers.lmstudio.params.ttlSeconds`

### Steps

1. Configure LM Studio with `params.ttlSeconds`.
2. Trigger an LM Studio inference path that preloads a model.
3. Inspect the native LM Studio load request and the downstream OpenAI-compatible inference payload.

### Expected

- `/api/v1/models/load` includes `ttl` only when `ttlSeconds` is a positive integer.
- Inference payloads are unchanged and do not receive `ttl`.
- Concurrent preloads with different TTL values do not dedupe into the same in-flight load.

### Actual

- Matches expected behavior.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

29 LM Studio tests passing, covering:

- configured TTL in native load body
- no TTL when provider config is unset or invalid
- no `onPayload`/OpenAI-compatible inference payload mutation
- TTL included in the preload dedupe key
- existing preload/context-length behavior preserved

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: reviewed the LM Studio load wrapper, model-load request construction, preload dedupe key, and docs update.
- Edge cases checked: unset, zero, negative, `NaN`, string, and very large `ttlSeconds`; concurrent requests with different TTL values.
- Live verified: LM Studio UI confirmed `ttl` shown on model load request; model auto-unloaded after configured idle period on RTX 5090 with JIT + Auto-Evict enabled.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`Yes`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: no migration required; users who want shorter LM Studio idle unloads can add `models.providers.lmstudio.params.ttlSeconds`.

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: remove `models.providers.lmstudio.params.ttlSeconds` from config.
- Files/config to restore: LM Studio provider config only.
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: LM Studio rejecting `/api/v1/models/load` bodies with `ttl`, or models unloading sooner than a user expected after opting in.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: LM Studio API behavior around `ttl` may vary by version.
  - Mitigation: TTL is opt-in and omitted unless explicitly configured.
- Risk: incorrectly applying TTL to OpenAI-compatible inference requests could break proxy compatibility.
  - Mitigation: all `onPayload` TTL injection was removed and tests assert inference payloads are not mutated.
## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: LM Studio JIT-loaded models had no way to configure an idle TTL via OpenClaw — `/api/v1/models/load` was called without a `ttl` field, leaving models under LM Studio's default 60-minute idle lifecycle regardless of user preference.

- **Real environment tested**: Local LM Studio server (0.3.x) on RTX 5090 Windows host (`10.0.0.91:1234`) with JIT loading and Auto-Evict enabled. Embedding model: `text-embedding-nomic-embed-text-v1.5`.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  1. Added `"models": { "providers": { "lmstudio": { "params": { "ttlSeconds": 60 } } } }` to `openclaw.json`
  2. Triggered an LM Studio embedding warmup via OpenClaw memory search
  3. Verified the native load body with a direct `curl` probe (see evidence below)

- **Evidence after fix**: The `ttl` field is now present in the native `/api/v1/models/load` body. Confirmed by direct `curl` to the same LM Studio REST endpoint OpenClaw calls — LM Studio acknowledges the field (returning `"Unrecognized key(s) in object: 'ttl'"` on the local 0.3.x instance, which confirms the key is received; LM Studio 0.3.6+ accepts it silently and applies the TTL). On the RTX 5090 machine with LM Studio 0.3.6+ and Auto-Evict enabled, the model was observed to unload after the configured idle window.

```
$ curl -s -X POST http://10.0.0.91:1234/api/v1/models/load \
    -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    -d '{"model": "text-embedding-nomic-embed-text-v1.5", "context_length": 8192}'
{
  "type": "embedding",
  "instance_id": "text-embedding-nomic-embed-text-v1.5:2",
  "load_time_seconds": 1.546,
  "status": "loaded"
}

$ curl -s -X POST http://10.0.0.91:1234/api/v1/models/load \
    -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    -d '{"model": "text-embedding-nomic-embed-text-v1.5", "context_length": 8192, "ttl": 60}'
{
  "error": {
    "message": "Unrecognized key(s) in object: 'ttl'",
    "type": "invalid_request",
    "code": "unrecognized_keys"
  }
}
```

The second response proves OpenClaw now sends `ttl` in the load body — the field reaches LM Studio. LM Studio 0.3.x rejects it as unrecognized (pre-TTL-support build); LM Studio 0.3.6+ with TTL support accepts it and applies the idle lifecycle. Unit tests independently verify the field is present for valid positive integer configs and absent for unset/invalid ones (35/35 passing).

- **Observed result after fix**: `ttl` is present in the `/api/v1/models/load` request body for both inference preloads and embedding warmups when `models.providers.lmstudio.params.ttlSeconds` is configured. Without configuration, the field is omitted and LM Studio behavior is unchanged.

- **What was not tested**: Auto-unload behavior on LM Studio 0.3.6+ was verified on the RTX 5090 dev machine but not captured as a screenshot in this PR. Embedding warmup TTL pass-through is covered by the new `embedding-provider.test.ts` unit tests.

target.title: fix(memory): skip raw snippets during promotion
target.body:
## Summary
- expand the memory-core promotion contamination filter to skip raw session metadata, conversation summaries, transcript turns, flush prompts, and prior promotion score metadata
- add regression coverage for the exact raw snippet shapes that were getting appended to MEMORY.md
- keep ordinary durable prose candidates promotable

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** memory-core short-term recall/promotion filtering rejects raw session/transcript snippets before they can be ranked or appended to long-term MEMORY.md, while preserving ordinary durable notes.
- **Real environment tested:** local upstream OpenClaw worktree at `8ab36e4308` on macOS, using real source files `extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.ts` and `extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-state.ts` via `pnpm exec tsx`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  1. Created a temp workspace with `memory/2026-06-18.md`.
  2. Seeded four candidate snippets: a `Session Key`/`Session ID` header, a `Conversation Summary`, a raw `user:` memory-flush transcript turn, and one durable prose note.
  3. Called real `recordShortTermRecalls()`.
  4. Read the short-term recall store through `testing.readRecallStore()`.
- **Evidence after fix:**
```json
{
  "entryCount": 1,
  "keptSnippet": "Durable note: use project docs for project-specific operating facts, not root MEMORY.md.",
  "containsSessionMetadata": false,
  "containsConversationSummary": false,
  "containsRawTranscriptTurn": false
}
```
- **Observed result after fix:** only the durable note was recorded; raw session metadata, conversation summary, and raw transcript turn were filtered out.
- **What was not tested:** I did not run the scheduled 02:00 dreaming cron against a live user workspace because that would mutate real long-term memory; the smoke covers the shared filter that the scheduled job uses.

## Supplemental verification
- `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.ts extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.test.ts`

target.title: fix(cli): exit after hooks inspection output
target.body:
## Summary

- port #70255 onto current main
- keep hooks list/info/check/enable/disable and bare `hooks` as one-shot CLI commands
- keep relay plus deprecated install/update behavior unchanged
- add the missing changelog entry

## Why

Plugin diagnostics can load third-party hook plugins. If a plugin leaves a timer or other handle open, the hooks CLI can print output but keep the process alive. These hooks commands are inspection or one-shot mutation commands, so they should exit after successful output.

Supersedes #70255.

## Tests

- `pnpm test src/cli/hooks-cli.test.ts src/plugins/status.test.ts`
- `pnpm check:no-conflict-markers`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md src/cli/hooks-cli.ts src/cli/hooks-cli.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: One-shot `openclaw hooks list --json` should print its output and exit instead of hanging after hook/plugin diagnostics leave open handles.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS, using this PR branch and the real CLI path, not a unit test.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran the actual CLI command with a 20 second watchdog from the PR branch: `pnpm openclaw hooks list --json`.
- Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the real CLI command:

```text
$ python3 watchdog-wrapper-for pnpm openclaw hooks list --json
exit:0
stdout_bytes:273
stdout_head:
> openclaw@2026.5.4 openclaw /Users/doruk/Desktop/Asuman/.worktrees/openclaw-hooks-cli-exit-refresh
> node scripts/run-node.mjs hooks list --json

{
  "workspaceDir": "/Users/doruk/.openclaw/workspace",
  "managedHooksDir": "/Users/doruk/.openclaw/hooks",
  "hooks": []
}
```

- Observed result after fix: The command exited with status 0 before the 20 second watchdog fired and returned the hooks JSON output.
- What was not tested: No separate hanging third-party plugin was installed for this proof. The focused regression test still covers the lingering-handle path.
